data.history: The Al Rughaib family appears to be established in Kuwait based on available contemporary evidence. The family has members engaged in various professional sectors including banking, agriculture, and sports. Nasser Al Rughaib works at the National Bank of Kuwait, representing the family's involvement in Kuwait's financial sector. Another family member, Nour Al-Rughaib, serves as a Project Manager at Palms Agro Production Co. and was educated at The Australian College of Kuwait, indicating the family's engagement with both local business and international education. The family also has representation in athletics, with Naser Al Rughaib participating in Muay Thai competitions, competing in the 3rd Kuwait Muay Thai Championship in April 2019.
data.description: A Kuwaiti family with members active in banking, agriculture, and sports sectors. Contemporary evidence shows their establishment in Kuwait's professional landscape.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains professional presence in Kuwait's banking sector through employment at National Bank of Kuwait, agricultural business through Palms Agro Production Co., and sports participation in national Muay Thai competitions.

data.history: The Zilkha family is a Jewish banking and commercial family of Iraqi origin that established one of the first regional banking networks in the Middle East. Founded by Khedouri (Khabouri) Abudi Zilkha (1884–1956) in Baghdad in 1899 as a small money-changing house, the business rapidly expanded into a regional financial institution. Starting in the late 1920s, the family opened branches across the region: Beirut in 1927, Damascus in 1935, and Cairo in 1937, creating what contemporary sources described as the first organized banking branch network in the area. Following the 1948 Arab-Israeli War, the family faced increasing restrictions and confiscations - their Baghdad assets were seized in 1950, and their Cairo branches were nationalized in 1956. This forced the family to relocate their financial operations to Europe and the United States, where they continued their activities in international finance and investment, establishing themselves as a prominent global financial family with significant business, philanthropic, and cultural contributions.
data.description: An Iraqi Jewish banking family that built the first regional banking network in the Middle East before relocating to Europe and America following asset confiscations in the 1950s. The family continues to be prominent in international finance, business, and philanthropy.
data.originStory: Khedouri Zilkha established a small money-changing house in Baghdad in 1899 with modest capital, which quickly transformed into a regional financial institution through strategic expansion across the Middle East.
data.modernStatus: The family operates internationally in finance, investment, and business, with notable ventures including Mothercare retail chain co-founding, Zilkha Energy in the US energy sector, and significant philanthropic contributions including the Zilkha Neurogenetic Institute at USC.

migrationPath.description: Forced migration due to asset confiscations following 1948 Arab-Israeli War

data.history: The Marafi family is one of the most prominent Persian merchant families in Kuwait, with origins tracing back to the 18th century. Marafi Behbehani was documented as one of the first merchants to settle in Kuwait in the 18th century, establishing the family's presence in the region. The family is part of the broader 'Ajam community of Kuwait - Kuwaiti citizens of Iranian descent who historically provided most of pre-oil Kuwait's economic needs through their extensive trade networks with Persian ports. In the 1950s, approximately 40-50,000 older generations of Persian immigrants, including the Marafi family, adopted Kuwaiti citizenship to maintain their economic stronghold in the country, successfully preserving their substantial economic influence. The family has maintained deep cultural ties to their Persian heritage, with Husseiniyat Marafi being among the oldest husseiniyas (Shia religious centers) in Kuwait, founded in 1905, where readings were initially conducted in the Persian language. In modern times, the family has successfully transitioned into contemporary Kuwaiti politics and business, with family members holding significant positions in government and maintaining their historical prominence in Kuwaiti society.
data.description: The Marafi family is a prominent Kuwaiti merchant family of Persian origin, tracing their presence to the 18th century when they were among the first Iranian merchants to settle in Kuwait. They have maintained significant economic and political influence while preserving their cultural heritage through religious institutions.
data.originStory: The Marafi family originates from Iran as part of the broader Persian merchant community. Marafi Behbehani was documented as one of the first merchants to settle in Kuwait in the 18th century, establishing the family's foundational presence in the region during Kuwait's pre-oil era when Persian ports provided most of Kuwait's economic needs.
data.modernStatus: The Marafi family maintains significant political and economic influence in modern Kuwait. Dawood Sulaiman Marafie serves as a prominent politician, elected to the National Assembly in 2023 and appointed as Minister of State for multiple portfolios in 2024, including National Assembly Affairs, Youth Affairs, and Communications. The family continues to be active in banking and global financial markets.

data.history: The Al Ibrahim family is a wealthy merchant family from Saudi Arabia, with members holding significant business interests and high-level political connections through marriage into the Saudi royal family. The family's most prominent political connection came through Al Jawhara bint Ibrahim Al Ibrahim, who became one of King Fahd's wives and mother to Prince Abdulaziz, King Fahd's youngest son. Her position as the king's fourth and favorite spouse provided significant influence, particularly after King Fahd's 1995 stroke when she assisted him with both personal and public affairs. The family's business prominence is exemplified by Waleed bin Ibrahim Al Ibrahim, born in 1962, who founded the Middle East Broadcasting Center (MBC Group) in London in 1991 with Saleh Kamel, creating the first independent Arabic satellite TV station. After completing his education in Oregon during the 1980s, Waleed first established ARA Productions and Television Studios (ARAvision) in Saudi Arabia before launching MBC. His media empire expanded to include multiple channels and Al Arabiya news network, launched in 2003 as an alternative to Al Jazeera. The family's influence extended through multiple marriages into prominent Saudi families - Maha Al Ibrahim married Prince Abdul Rahman (King Fahd's brother), while Mohdi Al Ibrahim married former Saudi education minister Khaled Al Angari. In November 2017, Waleed was detained during Saudi Arabia's anti-corruption crackdown at the Ritz-Carlton hotel but was released in January 2018 after being found innocent of wrongdoing. In 2018, the Saudi government acquired 60% of MBC Group while Waleed retained 40% ownership and his chairman position.
data.description: A prominent Saudi merchant family known for their media empire through MBC Group and significant political connections via marriages to the Saudi royal family, including King Fahd's wife Al Jawhara bint Ibrahim Al Ibrahim.
data.modernStatus: Major media conglomerate owners through MBC Group (40% owned by Waleed Al Ibrahim, 60% by Saudi government as of 2018). Family maintains high political connections through royal marriages and business influence across the Arab world.

data.history: The Qarmatians were an Isma'ili Shia militant movement led by an Arabized dynasty of Persian descent, centered in Al-Ahsa in Eastern Arabia. The dynasty was founded by Abu Sa'id al-Jannabi, who was of Persian origin from Jannabah in coastal Fars. In 899 CE, they established a religious state after Abu Sa'id captured Bahrain's capital Hajr and al-Hasa, making the latter his capital. The Qarmatians emerged from a split in the Isma'ili movement in 899, when they rejected the claims of Fatimid Caliph Abdallah al-Mahdi Billah to the imamate and clung to their belief in the coming of the Mahdi. Under Abu Tahir al-Jannabi (914-944), they launched devastating attacks across the region, most infamously sacking Mecca in 930 and stealing the Black Stone, which they held for ransom until 952. The Qarmatian state controlled vast fruit and grain estates worked by approximately thirty thousand Ethiopian slaves and operated a unique economic system with no taxes for residents and lead token money for local transactions. Their power began declining after 931 when Abu Tahir appointed a false Mahdi who lasted only 80 days before execution, severely damaging their credibility. After defeats by the Abbasids in 976, the Qarmatians retreated to local power, losing tributary income and coastal control. The final blow came in 1067 when Abdullah bin Ali Al Uyuni, supported by Seljuk forces, besieged their stronghold at Hofuf for seven years until they surrendered, ending Qarmatian rule and establishing the Uyunid dynasty.
data.description: An Isma'ili Shia militant movement of Arabized Persian origin that ruled Eastern Arabia from 899-1067 CE, most notorious for sacking Mecca and stealing the Black Stone in 930.
data.originStory: The founder Abu Sa'id al-Jannabi was of Persian origin from Jannabah in coastal Fars. The movement's name derives either from Hamdan Qarmat, an early convert, or from Aramaic/Arabic words meaning 'short-legged,' 'red-eyed,' 'secret teacher,' or relating to close writing script.
data.modernStatus: The Qarmatian dynasty ended in 1067 CE and no longer exists as a political entity.
data.nameEtymology: The name 'Qarmatian' has uncertain origins - it may derive from the surname of Hamdan Qarmat, from Aramaic meaning 'short-legged,' 'red-eyed' or 'secret teacher,' or from the Arabic verb qarmat meaning 'to make lines close together in writing' or 'to walk with short steps.' It can also refer to a type of Arabic script.

data.history: The Al Swailem family name appears in modern contexts within the Arabian Peninsula, with documented presence in Saudi Arabia. The research provides limited historical information about the family's origins or early development. A contemporary member, Fahad Hussain Al-Swailem, born in 1992, represents the family in professional football as a winger or defender for Saudi Arabian club Al-Ghottah. One historical reference mentions Mohammed Edris Abu Swailem, who was reportedly killed on July 12, 2014, during Israeli strikes in Gaza, though this may represent a different family line or branch. The available documentation does not provide sufficient detail about the family's tribal origins, migration patterns, or historical significance within the broader context of Arabian Peninsula genealogy.
data.description: A Saudi Arabian family with contemporary presence in the Kingdom, represented in modern times through sports and other civilian activities.

data.history: The Al Twaijri family's documented presence in the academic and medical fields can be traced through limited available research. W.A. Al-Twaijri, working alongside M.I. Shevell, has made notable contributions to pediatric neurology, specifically in the study of childhood periodic syndromes and migraine equivalents. Their research has focused on acephalgic migraines in children, which they classify as 'migraine equivalents' that can serve as predictors for the future development of typical migraines. This medical research represents the family's involvement in specialized healthcare and neurological studies, though the broader historical context of the family's origins and development remains undocumented in available sources.
data.description: The Al Twaijri family is documented through medical research contributions, particularly in pediatric neurology and migraine studies, though comprehensive historical information about the family's origins and broader activities is not available in current research.
data.modernStatus: Limited documentation shows involvement in medical research and pediatric neurology

notableFigures.biography: Medical researcher who has contributed to the understanding of pediatric acephalgic migraines and childhood periodic syndromes. Collaborated with M.I. Shevell in classifying migraine equivalents that can predict future development of typical migraines.

data.history: The Dallah family name appears to be connected to the traditional Arabic coffee culture of the Arabian Peninsula, with the dallah coffee pot being a central symbol of hospitality and cultural identity in GCC countries since at least the mid-17th century. The most prominent modern bearer of this name was Saleh Abdullah Kamel (1941-2020), a Saudi billionaire who founded the Dallah al Baraka Group (DBHC), one of the Middle East's largest conglomerates. Born in Mecca in 1941 and educated in various Saudi cities before earning a commerce degree from the University of Riyadh in 1963, Kamel built a business empire spanning financial services, banking, healthcare, real estate, manufacturing, and transportation. His company was instrumental in creating the Saudi Public Transport Company (SAPTCO) and he served as chairman of the Jeddah Chamber of Commerce and the Islamic Chamber of Commerce, earning the title 'father of contemporary Islamic finance.' Kamel was arrested in November 2017 during Crown Prince Mohammad bin Salman's anti-corruption crackdown but the research does not indicate the outcome of his case. He died on May 18, 2020, at age 79.
data.description: A family name associated with traditional Arabic coffee culture and modern Saudi business, most notably represented by billionaire businessman Saleh Abdullah Kamel who founded the Dallah al Baraka Group conglomerate.
data.modernStatus: The Dallah name is associated with major Saudi business interests through the Dallah al Baraka Group, a multinational holding company with investments across multiple sectors including finance, banking, healthcare, real estate, and transportation, though the current status following Saleh Kamel's 2020 death is unclear.
data.nameEtymology: The name appears connected to the dallah, a traditional Arabic coffee pot that has been used for centuries in Arabian Peninsula coffee rituals and is featured prominently in GCC cultural identity, including on public artwork and monetary coins.

data.history: The Al Fozan family established itself as a prominent business dynasty in Saudi Arabia, with their commercial activities dating back to 1969 when they founded Midar Building Materials Company, which became one of the largest building materials importers in the Kingdom, specializing in timber, reinforcement steel, metals, aluminum and insulation materials. The family's business empire expanded significantly under the leadership of Abdullah Abdul Latif Al Fozan, who joined the company in the early 1990s after graduating with a Bachelor's degree in Accounting from King Saud University in 1990. Under his guidance, the company underwent major horizontal and vertical expansion, diversifying into five main sectors: commercial, industrial, retail, real estate, and investments. In 2003, the family established the United Electronics Company (eXtra), their first retail venture, which was later listed on the Saudi stock exchange (Tadawul) in 2011 and became recognized as one of the top 100 brands in Saudi Arabia. The Al Fozan Holding Company now operates with a strategy spanning 70% within Saudi Arabia and 30% across other GCC countries, with subsidiaries including Retal Urban Development Company (2012), Nice Company for household supplies (2013), and various other industrial and commercial enterprises. The family has also established significant philanthropic initiatives through 'Al Fozan Community Service,' including the Abdul Latif Al Fozan Mosque Architecture Award founded in 2014 with an endowment of 60 million Saudi Riyals.
data.description: A prominent Saudi business family that built a diversified holding company empire spanning retail, manufacturing, real estate, and trade across the GCC region, with significant philanthropic activities in community development and Islamic architecture.
data.modernStatus: Major business conglomerate through Al Fozan Holding Company, with publicly traded subsidiaries like eXtra, extensive real estate and manufacturing operations, and significant philanthropic initiatives including the international Abdul Latif Al Fozan Mosque Architecture Award

data.history: The Pharaon family originated in Syria, with patriarch Rashad Pharaon born there in 1912 to a Syrian merchant father who had connections with the Al Saud family during both the Ottoman rule in Arabia and the formation period of Saudi Arabia. Rashad migrated to Saudi Arabia in 1936 to serve as the private physician of Ibn Saud, founder of Saudi Arabia, a position he held until Ibn Saud's death in 1953. During the reigns of three consecutive Saudi kings (Saud, Faisal, and Khalid), Rashad held various official posts including Saudi ambassador to Spain (1953-1954), minister of health (1954-1960), Saudi ambassador to France (1960-1966), and senior policy adviser to Kings Faisal and Khalid (1966-1982). The family became one of the richest families in Saudi Arabia through various assets and investments. Rashad's son Ghaith Pharaon became a prominent international businessman and financier, founding the Attock Group and serving as an investor in BCCI, though he later became a fugitive from US authorities due to banking fraud charges. The family established significant influence in both Saudi politics and international business, with Rashad being described as the closest person to King Faisal during his reign and the only non-royal official who participated in the king's meetings with foreign leaders.
data.description: A Syrian-origin family that rose to prominence in Saudi Arabia through Rashad Pharaon's role as Ibn Saud's personal physician and subsequent high-level government positions, eventually becoming one of Saudi Arabia's wealthiest families with significant business interests.
data.originStory: The family traces its origins to Syria, where Rashad Pharaon was born in 1912 to a Syrian merchant father who had established connections with the Al Saud family. Through these family connections and Shukri Quwatli's influence (future president of Syria), Rashad was able to migrate to Saudi Arabia in 1936 to serve as Ibn Saud's private physician.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains significant wealth and business interests, though Ghaith Pharaon died in 2017 while still wanted by US authorities. The family continues to operate businesses including Attock Group companies in Pakistan and other international ventures.

data.history: The Al Falasi (آل الفلاسي) are a prominent Emirati family whose most notable branch is the House of Al Falasi from which the ruling Al Maktoum family of Dubai descends. According to Wikipedia, Dubai was settled in 1833 by an offshoot of the Bani Yas tribe called the Falasi Al Maktoum. The Al Falasi are closely related to the House of Al Falahi (Āl Bū Falāḥ), another branch of the Bani Yas tribe from which the ruling Al Nahyan family of Abu Dhabi descends. The Bani Yas tribal confederation originally came to Abu Dhabi in the 18th century from Liwa Oasis, and the Al Falasi represent one of the sub-clans of this influential tribal group. In modern times, the Al Falasi family has produced prominent figures in UAE government and academia, including Dr. Ahmed Balhoul Al Falasi, who has served in multiple ministerial positions including Minister of Education (2022-2024) and currently serves as Minister of Sports and President of Higher Colleges of Technology since July 2024.
data.description: The Al Falasi are a prominent Emirati family, most notably known as the tribal origin of Dubai's ruling Al Maktoum family. They are a branch of the influential Bani Yas tribal confederation.
data.originStory: The Al Falasi are descendants of the Bani Yas tribe, with Dubai being settled in 1833 by an offshoot of the Bani Yas known as the Falasi Al Maktoum. The Bani Yas confederation came to the region from Liwa Oasis in the 18th century.
data.modernStatus: The Al Falasi maintain significant influence in UAE society and government. Dr. Ahmed Balhoul Al Falasi currently serves as Minister of Sports and President of Higher Colleges of Technology, and previously held positions as Minister of Education, Minister of State for Higher Education, and other senior government roles.

migrationPath.description: Migration of Bani Yas confederation from Liwa Oasis, with Falasi branch eventually settling Dubai in 1833

data.history: The Al Enezi family represents a prominent tribal lineage with documented presence across the Arabian Peninsula, particularly in Kuwait and Saudi Arabia. In Kuwait, the family has established a significant political presence, with Khalaf Al-Enezi serving as a member of the Kuwaiti National Assembly representing the second district from 1981 until his death in 2022. The family's political engagement has been characterized by independent positions and good relations with the royal family, with Khalaf working at the National Council before his parliamentary career. In Saudi Arabia, the Al Enezi lineage includes notable figures like Colonel Rabih Alenezi, a former senior intelligence officer who served in Saudi Arabia's General Directorate of Public Security for two decades before defecting in 2023. The family's modern history reflects the broader political and social dynamics of the Gulf region, with members taking significant roles in both supporting and opposing governmental policies across different countries.
data.description: Al Enezi is a prominent Arabian tribal family with documented political and security sector presence in Kuwait and Saudi Arabia, known for independent political positions and significant public service roles.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains significant political influence in Kuwait through parliamentary representation and has produced high-ranking security officials in Saudi Arabia, though some members have become dissidents in exile.

data.history: The Al Sanea family represents multiple distinct lineages in Saudi Arabia, with no confirmed connection between them based on available research. The most prominent modern figure is Maan Abdul Wahed Al-Sanea (born 1955 in Khobar), a Saudi businessman of Kuwaiti origin who founded the Saad Group conglomerate in 1980. His business empire encompassed construction, engineering, real estate, and financial services, but collapsed spectacularly in 2009 amid allegations of defrauding his wife's family, the Al Gosaibis. The case became one of the largest financial scandals in Gulf business history, involving lengthy liquidation processes including the notable Singularis Holdings case. Al-Sanea was arrested at his home in Saudi Arabia in October 2017. Separately, Rajaa al-Sanea (born 1981) represents a different branch of the family, being the daughter of doctors in Riyadh who gained international recognition as an author and dental professional. Her controversial novel 'Girls of Riyadh' published in 2005 caused significant social upheaval and was banned in Saudi Arabia for its frank depiction of young women's lives, leading to death threats and international media attention.
data.description: Multiple Saudi Arabian families sharing the Al Sanea surname, including a collapsed business dynasty of Kuwaiti origin and separate professional/literary branches, with no confirmed genealogical connections.
data.originStory: Maan Al-Sanea's family is documented as being of Kuwaiti origin, though the specific details of their migration to Saudi Arabia are not detailed in available sources.
data.modernStatus: The business branch suffered complete collapse with the 2009 Saad Group bankruptcy and Maan Al-Sanea's 2017 arrest. Other Al Sanea family members pursue careers in literature, academia, and healthcare, with no apparent connection to the disgraced business empire.

data.history: The Al Kooheji family is a prominent Bahraini family with significant involvement in business and politics. Based on the research, Kooheji Contractors was involved in the construction of major commercial projects including Fakhro Tower in Manama, indicating the family's established presence in Bahrain's construction sector. The family has maintained political engagement, with Hamad Al Kooheji entering politics and being elected to represent the First District in Muharraq Governorate in 2018. The family has also been involved in sports and social activism, with members like Ehsan Al Kooheji organizing youth initiatives such as the Bahrain Debate forum during the 2011-2012 period. Additionally, Hamza Kooheji has been associated with the KHK MMA fight team, demonstrating the family's diverse involvement across multiple sectors of Bahraini society.

data.history: The Al Khayarin family appears to be a prominent Qatari family with significant involvement in the country's military and political leadership. Based on the available information, they hold key positions in Qatar's governmental institutions. Brigadier General Mubarak Mohammed Al Kumait Al Khayarin currently serves as the commander of the Qatar Emiri Air Force, indicating the family's strong presence in the military establishment. In the political sphere, Hadi bin Saeed al-Khayarin serves as one of the Rapporteurs in Qatar's Consultative Assembly (Shura Council), demonstrating their continued influence in the country's legislative body. Additionally, Ali bin Saeed Al-Khayarin was a candidate for Deputy Speaker in the Shura Council's first sitting in 2021, though he was defeated by Hamda bint Hassan Al Sulaiti, further illustrating the family's active participation in Qatar's political processes.
data.description: Al Khayarin is a prominent Qatari family with significant representation in both military and political leadership positions. They currently hold key roles in the Qatar Emiri Air Force and the Consultative Assembly.

data.history: The Al Hinai family is a prominent Omani tribal family with documented political leadership dating to the mid-20th century. The most notable historical figure was Imam Ghalib bin Ali al-Hinai (c. 1912-2009), who served as the last elected imam of the Imamate of Oman from 1954 until the sultanate's conquest in 1959. Prior to becoming imam, Ghalib served as qadi (judge) of Rustaq and Nizwa, and later as treasurer of the imamate. His father, Ali bin Hilal al-Hinai, served as wali (governor) of Rustaq, establishing a tradition of administrative leadership in the family. The Al Hinai family played a central role in the Jebel Akhdar War (1954-1959), with Ghalib's brother Talib bin Ali serving as wali of Rustaq and becoming a key military leader in the imamate's resistance against Sultan Said bin Taimur and British forces. After the imamate's defeat, Imam Ghalib escaped to Saudi Arabia where he maintained a government-in-exile from Dammam and established an imamate office in Cairo. The family's involvement in modern corruption cases includes Juma al-Hinai, a Finance Ministry official who also headed Petroleum Development of Oman's tenders committee and was implicated in bribery scandals in 2010. Contemporary members include footballer Mohammed Mubarak Suwaid Al-Hinai, born in 1984, who has represented Oman internationally since 1999.
data.description: A prominent Omani tribal family known for producing the last imam of the Imamate of Oman and maintaining significant political and administrative roles in modern Omani society.
data.modernStatus: The Al Hinai family maintains presence in various sectors of Omani society, including government administration, sports, and business, though some members have been involved in corruption scandals in the petroleum sector.

data.history: The Al Siyabi family has a documented presence in Oman spanning several centuries, with particular prominence in religious scholarship and the judiciary within the Ibadi tradition. According to genealogical sources, they are described as one of the recognized Arab tribes in the Sultanate of Oman with a notable historical and scholarly presence, especially in religious and judicial fields. The family ruled Oman for approximately two centuries during the medieval period (367-546 AH/978-1151 CE), reflecting significant political importance. They were part of major tribal alliances, specifically the Hinawi-Ghafiri alliance system, which represented one of the largest tribal divisions in Oman. The family has produced numerous scholars, jurists, and judges, particularly within the Ibadi school of Islamic jurisprudence. In contemporary times, family members continue to hold important positions in government, especially in legal and religious spheres, and have played a role in spreading religious education and serving the Sharia judiciary in the Sultanate.
data.description: The Al Siyabi are a recognized Arab family in Oman with a prominent historical presence in religious scholarship and governance, having ruled Oman for nearly two centuries during the medieval period. They are particularly noted for producing numerous Islamic scholars, judges, and government officials within the Ibadi tradition.
data.originStory: According to genealogical sources in 'Isaf al-Ayan fi Ansab Ahl Uman', they trace their lineage to Shihab ibn al-Nuwayra ibn Amr ibn al-Harith ibn Zuhayr ibn Abi al-Nuwayra ibn Rabi'a ibn Murra ibn Zuhayr ibn Jashm ibn Bakr ibn Habib ibn Ghanim ibn Taghlib ibn Wa'il ibn Qasit ibn Hanab ibn Afsa ibn Du'mi ibn Jadila ibn Asad ibn Rabi'a ibn Nizar ibn Ma'd ibn Adnan. However, the Omani poet Ibn Adim al-Bahlani traces them to Quda'a, noting 'Where is the fire of war, Al Musayyab from Quda'a and the leader of the people is Zahran.'
data.modernStatus: Contemporary family members hold significant positions in Oman's government, particularly in legal and religious institutions. Notable modern figures include Sheikh Ahmed bin Hamad Al-Siyabi as Secretary-General of the Fatwa Office at the Ministry of Awqaf and Religious Affairs, Sheikh Ibrahim bin Said Al-Siyabi as former Deputy Chief Justice of the Supreme Court, and Dr. Said Al-Siyabi as an academic, playwright, and cultural activist.

data.history: The Al Sabbagh family are Arab Christians originally from Shuweir in Mount Lebanon, who became known as merchants and medical practitioners across the Levant and Egypt. The family name derives from 'sabbagh' meaning 'dyer' in Arabic, as Ibrahim's grandfather's generation were known as al-Sabigh (also meaning dyer), while his great-grandfather was Yusuf ibn Mar'i. Under Ibrahim's father Habib, the family established themselves as Melkite (Greek Catholic) merchants, moving from the inland areas of Syria to coastal towns including Beirut and Sidon around 1700. Habib and his brother Abbud relocated to Damietta, Egypt, where they joined a burgeoning community of Melkite merchants in this Mediterranean trading town. Ibrahim al-Sabbagh, born in Damietta around 1715, received education and medical training at the Mar Yuhanna monastery in Shuweir before establishing himself as a physician in Acre, Palestine in the 1740s. He rose to become the chief adviser and wazir to Daher al-Umar, the powerful sheikh and tax farmer of northern Palestine, wielding significant influence in economic and political affairs until both men were killed by Ottoman forces in 1775. The family's prominence in 18th-century Levantine politics and commerce, particularly in cotton trade with European merchants, represents their evolution from Lebanese mountain craftsmen to influential political and economic figures across the Eastern Mediterranean.
data.description: Arab Christian family from Mount Lebanon who became influential merchants and political figures in 18th-century Palestine and Egypt, most notably through Ibrahim al-Sabbagh's role as chief adviser to Daher al-Umar.
data.originStory: The Al Sabbagh family originated in Shuweir, Mount Lebanon, as Arab Christians belonging to the Melkite (Greek Catholic) Church. The family name derives from their ancestral occupation as dyers (sabbagh in Arabic), with Ibrahim's grandfather's generation being known as al-Sabigh before the family became known as Sabbagh under his father Habib.
data.modernStatus: The research shows historical figures from the 18th century and a modern Egyptian activist (Shaimaa al-Sabbagh, 1984-2015), but does not establish clear connections between historical and contemporary bearers of the name or current family status.
data.nameEtymology: Derives from Arabic 'sabbagh' meaning 'dyer', reflecting the family's ancestral occupation. The previous generation was known as 'al-Sabigh' (also meaning dyer) before becoming 'Sabbagh' under Ibrahim's father Habib.

data.history: The Al Thunayan family is a cadet branch of the House of Saud, descended from Thunayan bin Saud, who was the brother of Muhammad bin Saud, the founder of the Emirate of Diriyah. This makes them great-great-grandsons of Saud bin Muhammad, the founder of the Al Saud dynasty. The family's most prominent historical figure was Abdullah bin Thunayan Al Saud, who ruled as Emir of Nejd from 1841 to May 1843, making him the sole member of the Al Thunayan branch to achieve this position. He had been in southern Iraq under the protection of the Muntafiq tribe until 1841, when he successfully ousted his third cousin Khalid bin Saud with support from tribal leaders who regarded Khalid as illegitimate due to his Egyptian alliance. However, Abdullah's reign was short-lived - he was overthrown by another cousin, Faisal bin Turki, in May 1843 and died of poisoning while imprisoned in Al Masmak fortress in July 1843. Remarkably, one of Abdullah's sons was born on the same day he died and was named Abdullah after his father. This son, Abdullah bin Abdullah Al Saud, later moved to Constantinople where he served on the Ottoman Council of State and married a Turkish Circassian woman named Tazeruh Hanım. Their children established the modern Al Thunayan lineage, including Mohammad bin Abdullah Al Thunayan, who became a physician in the Ottoman army. The family gained renewed prominence through Mohammad's daughter Iffat bint Mohammad Al Thunayan, who married King Faisal of Saudi Arabia in 1932 and became known as Queen Iffat, playing a crucial role in Saudi women's education and establishing the first schools and university for women in the kingdom.
data.description: Al Thunayan is a cadet branch of the House of Saud descended from Thunayan bin Saud, brother of the dynasty's founder. They achieved historical prominence through Abdullah bin Thunayan who ruled as Emir of Nejd (1841-1843) and later through Queen Iffat, wife of King Faisal.
data.originStory: The Al Thunayan branch originated from Thunayan bin Saud, who was the brother of Muhammad bin Saud, the founder of the Emirate of Diriyah in the 18th century. This makes them a direct cadet branch of the ruling Al Saud family with roots in the original Saudi state established in Diriyah.
data.modernStatus: The Al Thunayan remain a cadet branch of the Saudi royal family. Members hold the title of Emir/Prince and continue to have connections to the ruling family through historical marriages, including Ahmed bin Abdullah Al Thunayan who served as an advisor to King Abdulaziz.
data.nameEtymology: Named after Thunayan bin Saud, brother of Muhammad bin Saud, the founder of the First Saudi State

data.history: The Al Seif family represents one of the prominent merchant and business families of Saudi Arabia, with their most well-documented lineage tracing to the town of Al-Attar in the Sudair region of central Arabia. The family descends from the sheikhs of Al-Attar and historically belonged to the Subai tribe, one of the prominent tribal groups of central Arabia. The modern Al Seif business empire was established by Khaled bin Musaed bin Saif bin Abdulaziz Al Saif (1953-2024), who founded Al Seif Engineering Contracting, a major construction and development company involved in large-scale infrastructure projects in Saudi Arabia and abroad. After earning a civil engineering degree from the American University of Beirut, Khaled expanded the family business into the Al Saif Group, a diversified conglomerate with interests spanning engineering, healthcare, and technology. The family gained international prominence through Princess Rajwa Al Hussein (born Rajwa bint Khaled bin Musaed bin Saif bin Abdulaziz Al Saif), who married Crown Prince Hussein of Jordan in June 2023, connecting the Al Seif family to Jordanian royalty. Through maternal connections to the Al Sudairi family, the Al Seifs maintain ties to the Saudi royal family, as Princess Rajwa's mother Azza Al Sudairi shares lineage with King Salman of Saudi Arabia's family.
data.description: A prominent Saudi Arabian merchant family from the Sudair region, descended from the Subai tribe, known for their major construction business empire and royal connections through Princess Rajwa's marriage to Jordan's Crown Prince.
data.originStory: The Al Seif family descends from the sheikhs of the town of Al-Attar in the Sudair region, which historically belonged to the Subai tribe, one of the prominent tribal groups of central Arabia.
data.modernStatus: Major business family operating the Al Saif Group, a diversified conglomerate with interests in engineering, healthcare, and technology. Connected to Jordanian royalty through Princess Rajwa Al Hussein's marriage to Crown Prince Hussein of Jordan in 2023.

data.history: The Al Suwaiket family is prominently represented in the modern business landscape of Saudi Arabia's Eastern Province through Mubarak Al Suwaiket Al-Hajri, a Saudi Arabian businessman who has made significant investments in real estate development. The family name gained particular prominence with the construction of the Suwaiket Tower (also known as Dhahran Tower) in Khobar, which stands as the tallest skyscraper in the Eastern Province at 200 meters with 46 floors. The tower project, costing approximately 350 million Saudi Riyals (about 80 million USD), was completed and opened on June 7, 2012, representing a new architectural milestone in the region. The development is strategically located at the intersection of the Khobar-Dammam road and Prince Sultan Street, about 40 minutes from King Fahd International Airport. The tower contains 291 furnished residential apartments of various sizes, ranging from 158 to 255 square meters, and includes amenities such as pools, gyms, and recreational facilities. This project established the Al Suwaiket family as significant contributors to the urban development and modernization of the Eastern Province's skyline.
data.description: The Al Suwaiket family is a prominent Saudi Arabian business family known for major real estate development in the Eastern Province, particularly through the construction of the region's tallest skyscraper.
data.modernStatus: Major real estate developers in Saudi Arabia's Eastern Province, with Mubarak Al Suwaiket owning the 200-meter Suwaiket Tower, the tallest building in the Eastern Province, completed in 2012 for 350 million SAR

data.history: Based on the available research, the Al Nemer family is represented by Ayatollah Sheikh Nimr Baqir al-Nimr (1959-2016), a prominent Saudi Shia cleric from Al-Awamiyah in Saudi Arabia's Eastern Province. The family appears to be part of the Shia Muslim community in the Eastern Province, with Al-Awamiyah serving as their hometown. Sheikh Nimr began his religious education locally before moving to Iran in 1980 to complete his studies at the Al-Qaim seminary in Tehran, primarily under Ayatollah Ali-Akbar al-Modarresi. After the seminary was closed, he moved to Damascus, Syria to continue his religious education. He returned to lead Friday prayers in Al-Awamiyah from 2008 until his execution in 2016. The family faced significant persecution from Saudi authorities, with Sheikh Nimr's brother Mohammad al-Nimr being arrested for tweeting about the death sentence, and two cousins, Miqdad and Mohammad al-Nimr, being killed by Saudi security forces during a raid in Awamiyah in March 2017.
data.description: A Saudi Shia clerical family from Al-Awamiyah in the Eastern Province, known for religious scholarship and advocacy for Shia rights, facing significant persecution from Saudi authorities.
data.modernStatus: The family has faced severe persecution from Saudi authorities, with Sheikh Nimr executed in 2016 and multiple family members killed or arrested for their activism and religious positions.

data.history: The Al Fawzan family traces its origins to the Al-Wadain branch of the Al-Shammās clan of the Ad-Dawāsir tribe, with roots in Ash-Shamasiyyah in the Al-Qassim Province near Buraydah. The family's most prominent figure is Salih al-Fawzan, born in 1935 in Ash-Shamasiyyah, who became orphaned at a young age and was raised by his extended family. He received his early Islamic education from local imām Ḥamūd ibn Sulaymān At-Tilāl before pursuing formal education at Al-Faysaliyyah School in Buraydah and later the Ma'had Al-'Ilmī. Al-Fawzan's career began as a primary school teacher before transitioning to religious scholarship and judicial roles. He advanced through various academic and religious positions, including teaching at the Imam Muhammad ibn Saud Islamic University, serving as director of the Higher Institute of Judiciary, and heading Saudi Arabia's Supreme Court of Justice. His appointment as Grand Mufti of Saudi Arabia on October 22, 2025, by King Salman represents the pinnacle of his religious career, making him the fourth person to hold this highest religious authority position in the kingdom.
data.description: The Al Fawzan family belongs to the Ad-Dawāsir tribe and is most notably represented by Salih al-Fawzan, who serves as the current Grand Mufti of Saudi Arabia since 2025. The family originates from Al-Qassim Province and has achieved prominence through religious scholarship and judicial service.
data.originStory: The Al Fawzan family belongs to the Al-Wadain branch of the Al-Shammās clan of the Ad-Dawāsir tribe, originating from Ash-Shamasiyyah in the Al-Qassim Province of Saudi Arabia, near the city of Buraydah.
data.tribalOrigin: Ad-Dawāsir tribe (Al-Wadain branch of Al-Shammās clan)
data.modernStatus: The family has achieved the highest religious authority in Saudi Arabia through Salih al-Fawzan's appointment as Grand Mufti in 2025. He serves as chairman of the Council of Senior Scholars and heads the Permanent Committee for Scholarly Research and Ifta, wielding significant influence in the kingdom's religious and legal affairs.

data.history: The Al Subai family traces its origins to the Subai tribe, one of the prominent tribal groups of central Arabia, historically based in the Sudair region. The family's connection to this tribal heritage is documented through Princess Rajwa Al Hussein's lineage - she is the daughter of Khaled Al Saif, whose family 'descends from the sheikhs of the town of Al-Attar in the Sudair region, which historically belonged to the Subai tribe.' The Subai tribe played a role in the political dynamics of the Arabian Peninsula, with historical records showing their involvement in various conflicts. During Abdullah bin Faisal Al Saud's reign in the 19th century, the Subai tribe was among his supporters during his struggle against his brother Saud, alongside the Al Suhul tribe from Al 'Aridh and the Qahtan of Najd. Additionally, there is evidence of intermarriage between the Al Saud ruling family and the Subai tribe, as Abdullah bin Jiluwi Al Saud 'wed a woman from the Al Subai tribe' in the early 20th century. The Al Saif branch of this tribal heritage has achieved significant prominence in modern Saudi business and society, with Khaled Al Saif becoming president of the Al Saif Group, a diversified conglomerate, and his daughter marrying into the Jordanian royal family in 2023.
data.description: The Al Subai are descendants of the Subai tribe, one of the prominent tribal groups of central Arabia based in the Sudair region. The family has achieved modern prominence through business success and royal connections, notably through Princess Rajwa Al Hussein's marriage to the Crown Prince of Jordan.
data.originStory: The Al Subai family descends from the sheikhs of the town of Al-Attar in the Sudair region, which historically belonged to the Subai tribe, one of the prominent tribal groups of central Arabia.
data.modernStatus: The family has achieved significant business and social prominence, with the Al Saif branch operating the Al Saif Group conglomerate and achieving royal connections through Princess Rajwa Al Hussein's marriage to Crown Prince Hussein of Jordan in 2023.

data.history: The Al Howeiti (Howeitat) are a tribe native to the northwestern region of Saudi Arabia, specifically in the area now designated for the NEOM megaproject in Tabuk Province. The tribe has historical roots in the lands near the northern tip of the Red Sea, in the region that extends toward the Gulf of Aqaba. In 2017, their traditional homeland became the site of Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman's ambitious NEOM project, leading to forced displacement of tribal members. The tribe has faced significant challenges since 2020, when activist Abdul Rahim al-Huwaiti was killed by Saudi security forces while resisting eviction orders. In October 2022, three members of the Howeitat tribe, including Shadli al-Howeiti (brother of Abdul Rahim), were sentenced to death by Saudi Arabia's Specialized Criminal Court for resisting displacement. An estimated 20,000 people from the area, primarily from the Howeitat tribe, are expected to be forcibly relocated to accommodate the planned city.
data.description: The Al Howeiti (Howeitat) are a native tribe of northwestern Saudi Arabia whose traditional homeland in Tabuk Province has become the site of the NEOM megaproject, leading to forced displacement and violent resistance since 2017.
data.modernStatus: The tribe faces forced displacement from their ancestral lands due to the NEOM project, with multiple members killed or sentenced to death for resistance, while an estimated 20,000 tribal members await relocation

data.history: The Al Saqqaf family (Arabic: السقاف) is a prominent Hadhrami Arab family tracing its lineage to the Ba'Alawi clan of the Banu Hashim in Hadhramaut. The family's documented lineage traces back to Abd al-Rahman al Saqqaf, a prominent Sheikh from Tarim, who was a 13th-generation descendant of Ahmad Al-Muhajir (born in Basra in 273 AH), the progenitor of the Ba'Alawi clan. The surname derives from the Arabic word 'mصنعة' (musna'a) meaning 'fortress' in the Sabaean language. The family has been historically active across Saudi Arabia, Yemen, Jordan, the United Arab Emirates, Malaysia, Indonesia, and Singapore, establishing significant political, diplomatic, and business influence across these regions through strategic marriages and business ventures. Notable family properties included the Al-Saqqaf Palace in Mecca (later purchased by King Abdulaziz) and the Perseverance Estate in Singapore. The family played crucial roles in regional politics, with members serving as foreign ministers, diplomats, chief justices, and business leaders throughout the 20th century. In Southeast Asia, the family became known as 'Alsagoff,' establishing trading companies, educational institutions, and maintaining significant real estate holdings including periods of ownership of the Raffles Hotel.
data.description: A prominent Hadhrami Arab family of the Ba'Alawi clan, historically active in politics, diplomacy, and business across the Arabian Peninsula and Southeast Asia. The family traces its lineage to the Banu Hashim through Ahmad Al-Muhajir.
data.originStory: The family traces its lineage to Abd al-Rahman al Saqqaf, a prominent Sheikh from Tarim in Hadhramaut. He was a 13th-generation descendant of Ahmad Al-Muhajir, who was born in Basra in 273 AH and is considered the progenitor of the Ba'Alawi clan of the Banu Hashim.
data.modernStatus: The family continues to maintain influence in various countries. In Yemen, members include journalists and human rights activists like Abdulaziz Al-Saqqaf (1951-1999) and Nadia Al-Sakkaf, former Minister of Information. The family maintains educational and religious institutions, particularly in Southeast Asia through the Alsagoff name.
data.nameEtymology: The surname Al Saqqaf derives from the Arabic word 'mصنعة' (musna'a) meaning 'fortress' in the Sabaean language, reflecting the family's origins in fortified settlements.

data.history: The al-Junaid family (Arabic: الجنيد) were Hadhrami merchants and traders who originally hailed from Hadhramaut. The family are a branch of the Ba 'Alawi sada, descendants of Sayyid Ahmad al-Muhajir al-Uraydi al-Husayni. Their business was initially based in Palembang under Dutch control, but due to Singapore's status as a free port, they found it profitable to migrate there. The patriarchs of the al-Junaid family, Syed Mohammed and Syed Omar, arrived in Singapore between 1820 and 1821. Upon arrival, Syed Omar became one of the most prominent Arab merchants in Singapore and made significant contributions to the colonial economy. As philanthropists, the family donated land and properties for community benefit, including establishing the Masjid Omar Kampong Melaka in 1820, which became the oldest surviving mosque in Singapore. The family continued their business activities well into the 20th century, establishing Madrasah Aljunied Al-Islamiah in 1927 and Toko Aljunied textile shop in 1940, which remains operational today.
data.description: A Hadhrami merchant family from the Ba 'Alawi sada lineage who migrated from Palembang to Singapore in the 1820s and became prominent Arab merchants, establishing the oldest mosque in Singapore and contributing significantly to the colonial economy.
data.originStory: The al-Junaid family were originally traders and merchants who hailed from Hadhramaut and are a branch of the Ba 'Alawi sada, descendants of Sayyid Ahmad al-Muhajir al-Uraydi al-Husayni. Their business was originally based in Palembang under Dutch control before they migrated to Singapore.
data.modernStatus: The family legacy continues through the operational Toko Aljunied textile shop on Arab Street established in 1940, and their historical contributions are commemorated through the Aljunied neighborhood and MRT station named after them.

data.history: The Al Shirazi family is a distinguished Shia clerical family that emigrated from Shiraz, Iran to Iraq in the 19th century, establishing themselves as prominent religious scholars in Karbala and Najaf. The family patriarch was Mirza Shirazi, a pioneer of the 1891 Tobacco Movement against British monopolies in Iran. His descendants, including Mirza Mahdi al-Shirazi, continued the family's religious scholarship tradition in Iraq. In 1971, due to persecution by the Ba'athist regime, family members including Muhammad al-Shirazi and Sadiq al-Shirazi were exiled from Iraq, settling first in Kuwait where they established important religious centers and seminaries. After the 1979 Iranian Revolution, many family members relocated to Qom, Iran, where they continued their religious activities. The family has produced multiple Grand Ayatollahs and maraji' (sources of emulation) and maintains significant influence among Shia communities globally, particularly through religious institutions in Kuwait, Iran, and among Saudi Arabian Shia populations in the Eastern Province.
data.description: A prominent Shia clerical family originally from Shiraz, Iran, who migrated to Iraq in the 19th century and later established important religious centers in Kuwait and Iran, producing multiple Grand Ayatollahs and influential religious scholars.
data.originStory: The Al Shirazi family originated from Shiraz, in southwestern Iran (Persia), and emigrated to Karbala, Iraq in the 19th century. The family traces its religious scholarly tradition to Mirza Shirazi, who was instrumental in the 1891 Tobacco Movement in Iran.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains significant religious authority in contemporary Shia Islam, with Grand Ayatollah Sadiq al-Shirazi serving as a marja' based in Qom, Iran. They operate religious institutions globally and maintain particular influence among Saudi Arabian Shia communities, with Sheikh Hassan al-Saffar representing their followers in Saudi Arabia's Eastern Province.
data.nameEtymology: Al Shirazi derives from Shiraz, a city in southwestern Iran, indicating the family's Persian origins from that region.

In modern Middle Eastern politics, the name surfaces in Lebanese political circles through figures like Misbah Ahdab, who was mentioned in connection with the Democratic Renewal Movement founded by Nassib Lahoud in 2001. data.history: Based on the research provided, Al-Mikhwah is one of the major cities in the Al-Baha Province of Saudi Arabia, specifically located in the Tihamah region - the lowland coastal area to the west of the Hejaz Mountains. The region that includes Al-Mikhwah is historically the homeland of the Ghamid and Zahran tribes, both branches of the Azd tribe. The Sarawat Mountains, which include the highlands of Al-Baha, were once inhabited by the Amalek (pre-Islamic extinct Arabs) until they were displaced by the Ghatareef, descendants of Al-Harith bin Abdullah bin Yashkur bin Mubashir bin Sa'b bin Dahman bin Nasr bin Zahran of the Azd tribe, who then settled the Azd in the Sarawat region. The area has ancient historical significance, with tribes tracing their origins to the ancient Arabian Mamlakat Saba' (possibly the Kingdom of Sheba), whose rule extended to areas presently known as Syria and Lebanon. Al-Mikhwah, along with Qilwah, represents one of the two major cities in the Tihamah coastal region of Al-Baha Province.
data.description: Al-Mikhwah is a major city in the Tihamah coastal region of Al-Baha Province, Saudi Arabia, situated in the traditional homeland of the Ghamid and Zahran tribes of the Azd confederation.
data.originStory: The research indicates that the broader Al-Baha region, including Al-Mikhwah, is inhabited by tribes that trace their origins to the ancient Arabian Mamlakat Saba' (possibly the Kingdom of Sheba). The area was historically settled by the Azd tribe after the Ghatareef displaced the pre-Islamic Amalek people from the Sarawat Mountains.
data.modernStatus: Al-Mikhwah is currently one of the two major cities in the Tihamah region of Al-Baha Province, serving as an important urban center in the coastal lowlands of southwestern Saudi Arabia.

data.history: The Al Mansouri family has produced several prominent figures in the modern UAE, particularly in military and space sectors. The family includes notable members such as Mariam al-Mansouri, the UAE's first female fighter pilot who graduated from Khalifa bin Zayed Air College in 2007 and led UAE airstrikes against ISIS in Syria in 2014. Another distinguished family member is Hazzaa Al Mansoori, who became the first Emirati astronaut and the first Arab to visit the International Space Station in 2019 as part of the UAE's 'Zayed's Ambition' mission. The family has connections to political figures, with Mariam al-Mansouri being related to Sultan bin Saeed Al Mansoori, described as an engineer and politician in the UAE. The Al Mansouri family represents the modern UAE's advancement in military aviation and space exploration, with multiple members serving as F-16 pilots in the UAE Air Force. Their achievements reflect the UAE's broader national ambitions in aerospace and defense sectors during the 21st century.

data.history: The Bin Sulayem (also written as bin Sulayem) family is one of Dubai's established business and political families, active since the early 20th century. According to Wikipedia, Sultan Ahmed bin Sulayem was born into this family in 1955, with his father serving as a key advisor to Dubai's ruling Maktoum family. The family gained prominence through their close association with Dubai's leadership and their involvement in the emirate's maritime and trade development. Sultan Ahmed bin Sulayem's career trajectory illustrates the family's evolution from government advisory roles to becoming central figures in Dubai's transformation into a global logistics and trade hub. Starting as a customs officer at Dubai's port in the late 1970s after graduating from Temple University with a degree in economics, he was appointed by Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum to chairman of the Jebel Ali Free Zone (JAFZA) in 1985. Under his leadership, JAFZA expanded dramatically from hosting 19 companies in the mid-1980s to roughly 7,500 by 2020, representing the family's role in Dubai's economic diversification strategy.
data.description: The Bin Sulayem family is one of Dubai's prominent business and political families, active since the early 20th century, with close ties to the ruling Maktoum family and significant involvement in Dubai's port, logistics, and free zone development.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains significant influence in Dubai's business sector through leadership positions in major state-owned enterprises. Sultan Ahmed bin Sulayem served as chairman and CEO of DP World (the world's third-largest port operator) until February 2026, and was chairman of the Ports, Customs & Free Zone Corporation. His son, Ahmed Sultan bin Sulayem, serves as chairman of the government-owned Dubai Multi Commodities Centre. However, Sultan Ahmed resigned from his executive positions in February 2026 due to his association with Jeffrey Epstein following the public release of the Epstein files.

data.history:
The Varkey family traces its origins to Kerala, India, where they belonged to the Kerala Syrian Christian (Nasrani) community. K.S. Varkey and his wife Mariamma were educators who made the pivotal decision to migrate to Dubai in 1959 when the emirate was still very undeveloped. K.S. Varkey worked for the British Bank of the Middle East (now HSBC Bank Middle East), while both parents taught English to local Arabs, including members of the royal family, establishing the family's foundation in education.

The family's transformation from educators to major business entrepreneurs began in 1968 when K.S. Varkey and Mariamma founded Our Own English High School in Dubai. Their son Sunny Varkey took over the school's operation in 1980 when local authorities required it to be housed in a purpose-built facility. At that time, the school had under 400 students, but Sunny expanded it significantly to serve the growing expatriate community whose children needed education separate from local schools reserved for native Arabs.

From this single school, Sunny Varkey built a global education empire. In 2000, he established Global Education Management Systems (GEMS), which became the largest operator of private kindergarten-to-grade-12 schools worldwide, with over 80 schools in more than a dozen countries. The Varkey family also diversified into healthcare through Welcare in 1984, though they sold their controlling interest in 2012 to focus on education. Today, the family operates through the umbrella organization Varkey Group, founded in 1979, and engages in significant philanthropy through the Varkey Foundation established in 2010.
data.description: The Varkey family are Kerala Syrian Christians who migrated from India to Dubai in 1959 and built a global education empire. Starting with a single school in 1968, they now operate GEMS Education, the world's largest private K-12 school network.
data.originStory: The Varkey family originated from Ranni, Kerala, India, where they were part of the Kerala Syrian Christian (Nasrani) community. K.S. Varkey and Mariamma Varkey were educators who moved to Dubai in 1959 when the emirate was still very undeveloped, initially working in banking and teaching English to local Arabs including royal family members.
data.modernStatus: The Varkey family controls GEMS Education, the world's largest private K-12 school operator with over 80 schools globally, and the Varkey Group umbrella organization. Sunny Varkey has a net worth of $3.5 billion according to Forbes and is a UNESCO Goodwill Ambassador who joined The Giving Pledge in 2015.
data.nameEtymology: Varkey is a variant of the Saint Thomas Christian (Nasrani) name Geevarghese which itself is a variant of George, commonly found among Christians of Kerala

data.history: The Maraziq (singular Al Marzooqi or Al Marzouqi) is a tribe of the United Arab Emirates traditionally associated with the settlement of Rams, north of Ras Al Khaimah. Together with the Tunaij tribe, the Maraziq population settled the western area of Rams, specifically the Bani Humud neighbourhood (freej) between Rams and Hulaylah. The late Islamic township of Rams consisted mostly of khaimahs - traditional small, stone-walled houses capped with gabled barasti (palm-frond) roofs. The Maraziq population had left the Rams area by the early 1950s and are today dispersed around the UAE. The Bani Humud area of Rams continued to be known as the Marzouqi neighbourhood even after the Maraziq families had departed. The Maraziq are believed to originally have migrated from Arabia and to have taken the name of the district they settled in Rams, which was originally populated by Iranians.
data.description: A UAE tribe traditionally associated with Rams settlement north of Ras Al Khaimah, believed to have migrated from Arabia and dispersed throughout the UAE by the 1950s.
data.originStory: The Maraziq are believed to originally have migrated from Arabia and to have taken the name of the district they settled in Rams, originally populated by Iranians.
data.modernStatus: The tribe is dispersed around the UAE, with notable contemporary figures including Mishal AlMarzouqi, a prominent mechatronics engineer and innovator who serves as director of R&D at Eanan (UAE's first drone manufacturing company) and has founded multiple technology companies.

data.history: The Al Bakri family traces its documented history to multiple distinct lineages across the Islamic world. The most historically significant branch descends from Abu Ubayd Abdullah ibn Abd al-Aziz al-Bakri (c. 1040-1094), an Arab Andalusian historian and geographer who belonged to the Arab tribe of Bakr. This medieval scholar was born in Huelva and spent his life in Al-Andalus, producing influential geographical works including the 'Kitab al-Masalik wa-al-Mamalik' in 1068. A separate Syrian branch emerged prominently in the modern era through Nasib al-Bakri (1888-1966), born in Damascus to the influential Ata al-Bakri family. This Damascus-based Al Bakri family claimed descent from the Islamic prophet Muhammad and were significant landowners in the al-Shaghour quarter, Qaboun, and Jaramana. They developed close ties with the Hashemites through Ata's service in Sultan Abdul Hamid II's Ottoman court, hosting Sharif Hussein and his sons including future King Faisal in their Damascus homes. Nasib al-Bakri became a key figure in Arab nationalism, co-founding the underground al-Fatat movement and serving as Faisal's personal secretary and envoy to the Druze during the Arab Revolt (1916-1920). He later played crucial roles in the Great Syrian Revolt (1925-1927), leading the brief rebel capture of Damascus in October 1925, and subsequently served in the Syrian Parliament from 1932-1946. A third branch appears in modern Saudi Arabia, represented by Saud al-Shuraim (born 1966), whose family originates from the Haraqees of the Banu Zayd tribe, though any connection to the other Al Bakri lineages remains unestablished.
data.description: The Al Bakri family represents multiple distinct lineages spanning medieval Andalusia, modern Syria, and Saudi Arabia, with documented branches including medieval Arab historians, Syrian nationalist politicians, and contemporary Saudi religious scholars, though connections between these branches remain unclear.
data.originStory: Multiple origin stories exist for different Al Bakri branches: the Andalusian branch belonged to the Arab tribe of Bakr, the Syrian Damascus branch claimed Hashemite descent from Prophet Muhammad, and the Saudi branch derives from the Haraqees of the Banu Zayd tribe.
data.tribalOrigin: Multiple tribal origins: Bakr tribe (Andalusian branch), Banu Zayd tribe (Saudi branch), claimed Prophetic lineage (Syrian branch)
data.modernStatus: The family maintains presence across multiple countries with varying roles: the Syrian branch historically held significant political influence through parliamentary representation and nationalist leadership, while the Saudi branch includes prominent religious scholars and former imams of Masjid al-Haram in Mecca.
data.nameEtymology: Al-Bakri derives from the Arab tribe of Bakr for the Andalusian branch, though etymology for other branches is not specified in available sources.

migrationPath.description: Al-Bakri moved from Huelva to Córdoba after his father was deposed by al-Mu'tadid of Seville

data.history: The Al-Rifai family is a widely dispersed Arab lineage historically connected to the Rifaʽi tariqa, a prominent Sufi order founded by the Iraqi saint Sayyid Ahmad al-Rifaʽi (d. 1183). The order originated in the marshlands of Iraq between Wasit and Basra, with Ahmad al-Rifaʽi teaching around Umm Ubayda near Wasit. From Lower Iraq, the Rifaʽi order spread to the Levant and Egypt by the later 12th and 13th centuries, with Syrian and Egyptian congregations emerging in urban centers. A Syrian branch associated with Abu Muhammad Ali al-Hariri (d. 1268) developed as the Ḥarīriyya. In Egypt, the order gained particular visibility during the Mamluk and Ottoman eras, culminating in Cairo's Al-Rifaʽi Mosque (constructed 1869-1912) which became both a devotional site and royal mausoleum for the Muhammad Ali dynasty. The surname al-Rifaʽi came to denote both affiliation to the Sufi order and, in many families, claimed descent from the saint. Today, Al-Rifai families are found across Iraq, Syria, Lebanon, Jordan, Palestine, and Egypt, with many preserving oral genealogies linking back to Sayyid Ahmad al-Rifaʽi.
data.description: The Al-Rifai family is a widely dispersed Arab lineage historically connected to the Rifaʽi Sufi order founded by Ahmad al-Rifaʽi in 12th-century Iraq. The family spread throughout the Middle East alongside the expansion of the Sufi order, with notable presence in Iraq, Syria, Lebanon, Jordan, Palestine, and Egypt.
data.originStory: According to Sufi biographical traditions, the Al-Rifai lineage traces back to Ahmad al-Rifaʽi (1119-1183), who lived and taught around Umm Ubayda near Wasit in Iraq and founded the Rifaʽi Sufi order. Standard hagiographic accounts describe him as a Husayni Sayyid descended from Imam Musa al-Kazim, though modern historians treat such long genealogical chains with methodological caution.
data.modernStatus: Contemporary Al-Rifai family members hold prominent positions across the Middle East, including multiple Prime Ministers of Jordan (Samir al-Rifai, Zaid al-Rifai, Samir Zaid al-Rifai), Syria's current Grand Mufti Osama al-Rifai, and various ministers, diplomats, and professionals across Iraq, Syria, Lebanon, Egypt, and Jordan.
data.nameEtymology: al-Rifaʽi is a nisba derived from the eponymous saint Ahmad al-Rifaʽi and his order, the Rifaʽiyya (also known historically as al-Bata'ihiyya) from the Iraqi marshlands. In Egypt and the Levant, the surname came to denote both affiliation to the Sufi order and claimed descent from the saint.

migrationPath.description: Spread of Rifaʽi order from Iraq to Levant and Egypt by later 12th and 13th centuries

data.history: The Al Thuwaini family emerged as a prominent branch of the Al Bu Said dynasty of Oman, tracing their lineage to Thuwaini bin Said (1821-1866), the Sultan of Muscat and Oman from 1856 to 1866. Thuwaini was the third son of Said bin Sultan and served as his father's representative in Oman while Said was often in Zanzibar. When Said bin Sultan died in 1856, a succession dispute arose between his sons, leading to British mediation that divided the empire - Thuwaini became Sultan of Muscat and Oman, while his brother Majid became Sultan of Zanzibar. Thuwaini's reign was marked by financial difficulties as Zanzibar ceased paying the agreed annual tribute of 40,000 Maria Theresa thalers, leaving Oman in a precarious economic position. His rule ended tragically in 1866 when he was reportedly killed by his own son, Salim bin Thuwaini. The Al Thuwaini lineage continued through various branches, with descendants maintaining significant positions within Omani royal and governmental circles. Notable modern descendants include Thuwaini bin Shihab Al Said (1924-2010), who served as Deputy Prime Minister and was considered a potential successor to Sultan Qaboos, and various members who have held diplomatic and administrative roles in the modern Sultanate of Oman.
data.description: The Al Thuwaini family is a prominent branch of Oman's ruling Al Bu Said dynasty, descended from Sultan Thuwaini bin Said who ruled Muscat and Oman from 1856-1866. They have maintained significant political and administrative roles in modern Oman.
data.originStory: The Al Thuwaini branch originates from Sultan Thuwaini bin Said (1821-1866), third son of Said bin Sultan of the Al Bu Said dynasty. The family name derives from their founding ancestor Thuwaini, who became Sultan of Muscat and Oman following the 1856 division of his father's empire.
data.modernStatus: The Al Thuwaini family maintains prominent positions in modern Omani government and royal circles, with members serving in diplomatic and administrative roles within the Sultanate of Oman.
data.nameEtymology: The family name Al Thuwaini derives from their ancestor Sultan Thuwaini bin Said, whose name became the patronymic identifier for his descendants within the broader Al Bu Said dynasty.

data.history: The research provides limited information about the Al Qabandi family, with the most significant reference being to Asrar Al-Qabandi, a prominent female resistance leader during the Iraqi invasion and occupation of Kuwait from 1990-1991. During the occupation, she helped people flee to safety, smuggled weapons and money into Kuwait as well as disks from the Ministry of Civil Information to safety, cared for many wounded by the war, and destroyed monitoring devices used by Iraqi troops. She was captured and subsequently killed by Iraqi troops in January 1991, making her a martyr of the Iraqi invasion. Her story represents the broader participation of Kuwaiti women in the resistance movement against the Iraqi occupation, which helped undermine the occupation's legitimacy and contributed to the eventual liberation of Kuwait.
data.description: A Kuwaiti family known primarily through Asrar Al-Qabandi, a female resistance leader martyred during the 1990-1991 Iraqi occupation of Kuwait.

data.history: The Al Shammari family belongs to the Shammar tribe, a major Qahtanite Arab confederation descended from the Tayy who migrated from Yemen to northern Arabia in the second century CE. Under the leadership of Usma bin Luai, the Tayy invaded the mountains of Ajā and Salma in 115 CE, displacing the Banu Assad and Banu Tamim. These mountains were later renamed Jabal Shammar in the 14th century when the tribe adopted the name Shammar. The tribe established the city of Ha'il as their traditional seat of power and became a dominant force in central and northern Arabia. During their golden age around the 1850s, the Shammar ruled vast territories from Riyadh to the Syrian frontiers and Upper Mesopotamia. The House of Rashid, a Shammar dynasty centered in Ha'il, became the most formidable enemies of the House of Saud, ruling the Emirate of Jabal Shammar from 1836 until their defeat by Ibn Saud in 1921. Following the Saudi conquest of Ha'il, many Shammar fled to Syria, Iraq, and other neighboring countries, though some remained and integrated into the new Saudi state.
data.description: Al Shammari is a prominent Arab family belonging to the Shammar tribe, one of the largest and most influential Arab tribal confederations originally from Yemen who became the rulers of northern Arabia through the Rashidi dynasty.
data.originStory: The Shammar trace their origins to the Tayy tribe, a Qahtanite confederation that migrated from Yemen to northern Arabia in the second century CE. Led by Usma bin Luai in 115 CE, they conquered the mountains of Ajā and Salma from the Banu Assad and Banu Tamim, establishing their dominion in what became known as Jabal Shammar.
data.modernStatus: The Al Shammari family remains widely distributed across the Gulf region, with members holding significant positions in various countries. Notable modern figures include Mohamed Al-Deayea, the renowned Saudi goalkeeper, and various political and business leaders across Kuwait, Qatar, and other Gulf states.

data.history: The research materials provide extensive information about various Sayyid figures and religious scholars, but contain no specific information about a family or lineage called "Sayyid Rajab." The sources discuss individual scholars with Rajab in their names or who died in the month of Rajab, such as Talib Pasha bin Rajab Al-Naqib Al-Refa'i, an Iraqi politician who became the first Minister of Interior in Iraq, and various religious figures whose deaths are commemorated in the month of Rajab according to Shia traditions. However, these references do not establish a coherent family history or genealogical lineage for a specific "Sayyid Rajab" family. The materials focus primarily on religious scholarship, political figures, and tribal histories from various regions including Yemen, Iraq, and the broader Islamic world, but do not contain sufficient information to construct a verified family history for the Sayyid Rajab lineage.
data.description: No verified information available about a specific Sayyid Rajab family lineage in the provided research materials.

data.history: Based on the available research, there are several unrelated individuals and entities with variations of the name 'Al Rabea' or 'Rabea', but no comprehensive information about a specific Gulf family lineage called 'Al Rabea'. The research includes Rabi' ibn Hadi 'Umayr al-Madkhali (1933-2025), a prominent Saudi Arabian Islamic scholar who was born in the village of Banu Shabil and became a professor at the Islamic University of Madinah. Al-Madkhali was known for his works in hadith studies and his opposition to the Muslim Brotherhood, eventually becoming a strong supporter of the Saudi government. The research also mentions Rabea Mechernane (born Rabea Kerzabi), an Algerian-Qatari women's rights activist who served as Algeria's minister of national solidarity and family in the 1990s before joining the court of Qatar's Emir Hamad bin Khalifa Al Thani in 2002. However, these individuals appear to represent separate lineages rather than a unified Al Rabea family with documented Gulf origins and genealogical connections.
data.description: No unified Al Rabea family history is documented in the available research, which only covers unrelated individuals with similar names from different regions and backgrounds.
data.modernStatus: No documented unified family status available

data.history: The Al Zankawi family is documented as a Kuwaiti family with a strong tradition in athletics, particularly track and field events. The family's athletic legacy spans multiple generations, with Mohammad Al-Zinkawi competing in shot put at three Olympic Games (1976, 1980, and 1988), establishing the family's presence in international athletics. This was followed by Ali Mohammed Al-Zenkawi, who specialized in hammer throw and achieved a personal best of 79.74 metres in 2009. The most recent generation is represented by Essa Mohammed Gharib Al-Zankawi, born in 1992, who has dual Kuwaiti/Hungarian heritage and competes in discus throw, winning medals at Asian Championships and Games while serving as Kuwait's flag bearer at the 2018 Asian Games opening ceremony.
data.description: The Al Zankawi family is a Kuwaiti family known for their multi-generational involvement in athletics, particularly track and field throwing events. The family has produced Olympic competitors and medalists who have represented Kuwait internationally from the 1970s through the present day.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains prominence in Kuwaiti athletics, with members continuing to represent Kuwait in international competitions and serving in ceremonial roles such as flag bearer at major sporting events.

data.history: The Al Sumait family is documented as having historical connections to both Kuwait and Arabia, with members appearing in significant political and philanthropic roles. Abdul Rahman Al-Sumait, a notable Kuwaiti philanthropist, belonged to the Subay' tribe, which is of North Arabian (Adnanite) stock and traces its lineage to the ancient Banu 'Amir tribe. The Subay' tribe historically roamed central Nejd around Riyadh and established settlements, with some sections moving to various Gulf states including Kuwait. In contemporary times, Nasser Al-Sumait serves as Kuwait's Minister of Justice, appointed in November 2024, demonstrating the family's continued involvement in Kuwaiti governance. During the 1990 Iraqi invasion of Kuwait, Yahya F. Al-Sumait served as Kuwait's housing minister and played a role in supporting the resistance movement against Iraqi occupation.
data.description: Al Sumait is a family with documented presence in Kuwait, connected to the Arabian Subay' tribe, with members serving in government positions and philanthropy.
data.originStory: The family originates from the Subay' tribe, which is of North Arabian (Adnanite) stock and traces its lineage to the ancient Banu 'Amir tribe that dominated central Arabia in the medieval period.
data.modernStatus: Active in Kuwaiti politics with Nasser Al-Sumait currently serving as Minister of Justice, and historically involved in philanthropy and government service.
