data.history: The Al Khudairi family appears to be a well-established Baghdadi family with roots in late 19th century Iraq. The earliest documented reference shows Abd al-Jabbar al-Khudairi as a merchant who established a distinguished heritage house on al-Rasheed Street in Baghdad in the late 19th century. This house later became notable for its architectural elements, woodwork, and shanasheel, eventually being converted into the Abd al-Karim Qasim Museum in 2015. The family maintained prominence in Baghdad society, with members establishing shrines in the al-Ghazali cemetery connected to the mausoleum of al-Gilani in the Bab al-Sheikh area. In the modern era, the family has achieved significant political prominence in Saudi Arabia, with Abdulaziz bin Abdullah Al Khudairi serving as Saudi Minister of Culture and Information from December 2014. The family also has connections to Iraqi academic circles through marriage, as evidenced by Nadra Al Khudairi's marriage to Iraqi academic and PFLP member Basil Al Kubaisi.
data.description: A prominent family with documented presence in Baghdad since the late 19th century as merchants, later achieving political prominence in Saudi Arabia through ministerial positions.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains political prominence in Saudi Arabia, with Abdulaziz bin Abdullah Al Khudairi having served as Saudi Minister of Culture and Information (2014-present as of the research). They also maintain historical ties to Baghdad through heritage properties.

data.history: The Al Shuraim family has documented origins within the Shammar tribe of Saudi Arabia, specifically from the Haraqees section of the Banu Zayd tribe. The family's most prominent historical figure is Saud ibn Ibrahim ibn Muhammad al-Shuraim, born in 1966 in Riyadh, who became a renowned Quran reciter and imam of Masjid al-Haram in Mecca. The family's tribal connections extend to significant historical alliances, with Asi bin Shuraim Al Shammari serving as sheikh of the southern part of the Shammar tribe and becoming a prominent supporter of King Abdulaziz during the formation of Saudi Arabia. Through marriage alliances, the Al Shuraim became connected to the Saudi royal family when Fahda bint Asi Al Shuraim married King Abdulaziz and became the mother of King Abdullah. The family has also established connections with other Gulf ruling families, notably through Sheikha Hessa bint Faisal bin Muhammad bin Shuraim Al Marri's marriage to King Hamad bin Isa Al Khalifa of Bahrain. The Al Shuraim maintain their tribal heritage while achieving prominence in religious, academic, and political spheres within Saudi Arabia.
data.description: Al Shuraim is a Saudi Arabian family of the Shammar tribe, known for producing prominent religious scholars and maintaining significant connections to the Saudi royal family through marriage alliances. They have achieved notable status in religious leadership and academic positions within the Kingdom.
data.originStory: The Al Shuraim family originates from the Haraqees section of the Banu Zayd tribe, which is part of the larger Shammar tribal confederation of Saudi Arabia. The family traces its leadership role to Asi bin Shuraim Al Shammari, who served as sheikh of the southern part of the Shammar tribe.
data.tribalOrigin: Shammar tribe (Haraqees of Banu Zayd section)
data.modernStatus: The family maintains prominent positions in Saudi religious and academic institutions. Saud Al-Shuraim served as imam of Masjid al-Haram until his retirement in 2022, and the family continues to hold academic positions at universities including Umm al-Qura University and University Bakri bin Ma'tooq.

She was among four women appointed to the council, alongside Muneera Al-Amir, Alyaa Al-Farsi, and Farah Al-Ruomi, as well as two male appointees, Ismael Behbehani and Abdulateef Al-Deei. The appointment followed the May 21, 2022 municipal elections where ten members were elected to the Municipal Council. The research contains no information about the family's origins, tribal affiliations, historical background, or other members beyond this single contemporary political appointment.

data.history:
The Algosaibi family is one of the oldest and richest trading families of Saudi Arabia and Bahrain, with their origins tracing back to Najd. The family's modern commercial empire was established by Hamad Ahmad Al Gosaibi in the late 1940s when he formed a trading company offering money exchange services in the growing coastal town of Al Khobar. His three sons - Ahmad, Abdulaziz, and Sulaiman - came together to establish 'Ahmad Hamad Algosaibi and Bros.' (AHAB), which became one of the most respected business houses in the Middle East.

The company expanded rapidly from its original trading and money exchange operations to include some of the world's most lucrative Pepsi Cola bottling franchises, oilfield services for Saudi Aramco, real estate, hospitality, food & beverages, and investments in banking, insurance, shipping, and manufacturing. The family reorganized their operations into a holding company structure, with AHAB being a general partnership owned by the heirs of the three founding brothers, each owning one-third of the business.

The Algosaibi family produced one of Saudi Arabia's most prominent figures in Ghazi Abdul Rahman Al Gosaibi (1940-2010), who became a distinguished politician, diplomat, technocrat, poet, and novelist. Born in Hofuf in Al Ahsa province to this wealthy merchant family, Ghazi served in numerous high-level government positions including Minister of Industry and Electricity (1975-1983), founding chairman of SABIC, Minister of Health (1983-1984), Ambassador to Bahrain and the UK, and Minister of Labor (2004-2010). His career was marked by his technocratic approach to governance and his advocacy for reform and modernization in Saudi Arabia.
data.description: The Algosaibi family is one of the oldest and richest trading families of Saudi Arabia and Bahrain, of Najdi origin, who built a major business empire through AHAB and produced prominent political figure Ghazi Abdul Rahman Al Gosaibi.
data.originStory: The family was of Najdi origin, with their commercial empire beginning when Hamad Ahmad Al Gosaibi established a trading company in the late 1940s in Al Khobar, originally offering money exchange services.
data.modernStatus: AHAB remains a major family-owned business group with vast real estate holdings, hotels, food & beverage chains (CaliBurger, Popeyes, Vanellis), shipping, trading and manufacturing ventures, with corporate partnerships including Jotun and Crown Holdings throughout the Middle East.

migrationPath.description: Commercial expansion from Najdi origins to Eastern Province coastal trading hub

data.history: The Behbahani family has deep historical roots tracing back to the city of Behbahan in Khuzestan province, Iran, which was built on the ancient Elamite city of Arrajan. The name 'Behbahan' derives from Persian, meaning either 'good palace/house surrounded by gardens' or potentially 'better than tents' following earthquakes that destroyed the ancient city. One of the most significant early members was Marafi Behbahani, who was among the first Iranian merchants to settle in Kuwait in the 18th century, establishing the family's presence in the Gulf region. The Behbahanis became part of the 'Ajam community in Kuwait - Kuwaiti citizens of Iranian descent who historically formed a significant merchant class. In the pre-oil era, the family played crucial roles in Kuwait's economic development, with Yusuf Behbehani building the first hotel in Kuwait City and Murad Behbehani founding Kuwait Television (KTV) before it was nationalized. The family maintained substantial economic influence even as they adopted Kuwaiti citizenship in the 1950s along with approximately 40-50,000 other Persian immigrants. Notable religious figures include the 18th-19th century scholar Wahid Behbahani, who led the Usuli school against the Akhbari movement in Shia Islam, and Seyyed Abdollah Behbahani, a prominent leader in Iran's Constitutional Revolution who was assassinated in 1910.
data.description: The Behbahani family is a prominent Persian merchant and scholarly family originally from Behbahan, Iran, with significant branches in Kuwait as part of the 'Ajam community, known for their contributions to Gulf commerce, media, and Shia religious scholarship.
data.originStory: The family traces its origins to the city of Behbahan in Khuzestan province, Iran, built on the ruins of the ancient Elamite city of Arrajan which was destroyed by earthquakes and rebuilt by Sasanian King Kavadh I (488-531 CE). The name Behbahan means 'good palace surrounded by gardens' in Persian, reflecting the region's agricultural heritage.
data.modernStatus: The Behbahani family maintains significant presence in Kuwait as part of the 'Ajam community, with members involved in business, media, sports, and academia. In Iran, family members have held high government positions, including Hamid Behbahani who served as Iran's Minister of Roads and Transportation (2008-2011).
data.nameEtymology: Behbahani derives from the city of Behbahan, where 'beh' means 'good' and 'bahan' means palace or big house surrounded by orchards and gardens, reflecting the region's agricultural character.

data.history:
Jawad Salem Al Arrayed was born in 1940 (or 1941 according to another source) in Manama, Bahrain, and became one of the most prominent legal and political figures in modern Bahrain. He obtained degrees in English literature from Cairo University and law from Leeds University, establishing himself as one of the highest-ranking Bahrainis educated and trained as a lawyer. His distinguished career in public service began in 1969 as a public prosecutor and evolved through numerous ministerial positions over more than four decades.

Al Arrayed's career trajectory demonstrates the family's integration into Bahrain's modern governmental structure. He served in various capacities including labor and social affairs minister (1971), minister of state for cabinet affairs (1972-1982), health minister (1982-1995), and minister of state for municipalities and environmental affairs (1995). His legal expertise proved crucial in Bahrain's successful defense of its sovereignty over the Hawar Islands in the International Court of Justice case against Qatar.

The family's political prominence reached its peak when Jawad Al Arrayed was appointed Deputy Prime Minister in December 2006, replacing a member of the royal family, Abdullah bin Khalid Al Khalifa. This appointment came following the victory of the Shia Islamist party Al Wefaq in parliamentary elections, demonstrating the family's continued relevance in Bahrain's evolving political landscape. He also served as Minister of Justice (2002-2005) and maintained his role as president of the ministerial committee for legal affairs, effectively serving as General Counsel to the Cabinet.
data.description: The Al Arrayed family is represented by Jawad Salem Al Arrayed, a prominent Bahraini lawyer and former Deputy Prime Minister who served in various ministerial positions from 1971 onwards. The family has achieved significant political prominence in modern Bahrain's governmental structure.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains high political status through Jawad Al Arrayed's role as President of the Ministerial Committee for Legal Affairs and former Deputy Prime Minister. He previously held numerous ministerial positions including Health, Justice, and Municipal Affairs, demonstrating continued influence in Bahrain's government.

data.history: The Al Doseri family name appears in various contexts across the Arabian Gulf region, with documented members in both Bahrain and other Gulf states. The research provides specific evidence of at least one notable Al Doseri individual: Jamal Ahmed Al-Doseri, born in 1970, who was a Bahraini former cyclist. This suggests the family has been established in Bahrain for several decades, though the exact timeline of their settlement or origins is not detailed in the available sources. Another reference appears in Arabic sources mentioning Mamdouh Hussein Issa Al-Doseri, born August 4, 1971, described as a former Bahraini cyclist who competed in team time trials at the 1992 Summer Olympics in Barcelona, Spain, indicating the family's continued presence and participation in Bahraini society through sports.

The most prominent figure is Mishal Al-Ahmad Al-Jaber Al-Sabah, the current Emir of Kuwait, who belongs to the Al-Sabah ruling dynasty of Kuwait. Born in 1940, he is the son of Ahmad Al-Jaber Al-Sabah and spent most of his career in Kuwait's security apparatus before becoming Crown Prince in 2020 and Emir in 2023. The research also mentions other unrelated individuals named Mishal, including British journalist Mishal Husain of Pakistani origin, Palestinian politician Khaled Mashal, and Emirati businessman Mishal Hamed Kanoo from the Kanoo merchant family. However, these represent distinct lineages rather than branches of a single 'Mishal family'.

data.history: The Al Fadhel family is a branch of the Bani Utbah through Lieutenant General Adel al-Fadhel's grandfather Fadhel bin Khalifa Al-Khalifa Al-Atabi. The family name also appears prominently in early Bahraini history, where Ahmed bin Muhammad bin Khalifa (the founder of the Al Khalifa dynasty) had a daughter named Amna bint Ahmed who was married to Rashid Al Fadhil, described as a distant relation of Sheikh Ahmed. Their son, Abdulrahman Al Fadhel, played a crucial role in Bahrain's history by regaining Bahrain after it was briefly occupied by the Wahhabis around 1809. The family has maintained significant positions in Bahraini government and security services, with Adel al-Fadhel serving in various high-ranking roles including head of the National Security Agency and Deputy Interior Minister. The Al Fadhel name appears to be well-established in Bahraini society, with connections to both the ruling family and important political positions spanning from the early conquest period through to modern times.
data.description: Al Fadhel is a Bahraini family branch of the Bani Utbah, with historical connections to the Al Khalifa ruling family and prominent roles in modern Bahraini government and security services.
data.originStory: The family is considered a branch of the Bani Utbah tribe through the lineage of Fadhel bin Khalifa Al-Khalifa Al-Atabi, grandfather of Lieutenant General Adel al-Fadhel.
data.modernStatus: The family holds prominent positions in Bahraini government, particularly in security and interior affairs, with Lieutenant General Adel al-Fadhel serving as head of the National Security Agency and Deputy Interior Minister.

data.history: Based on the research provided, the Al Safar family appears to be a prominent Persian merchant family that held significant influence in 19th century Bahrain. The research specifically mentions that among the most prominent great trading families that emerged during Bahrain's economic prosperity in the mid-19th century were 'the Persian Al Safar family, who held the position of Native Agents of Britain in the 19th century.' The Al Safar enjoyed an 'exceptionally close' relationship with the ruling Al Khalifa clan from 1869, though notably the al-Khalifa never intermarried with them, which has been speculated to be for political reasons to limit the Safars' influence with the ruling family, and possibly for religious reasons because the Safars were Shia. The family's wealth and prominence came during a period when Bahrain transformed from a tribal trading centre into a modern state, attracting large numbers of Persian, Huwala, and Indian merchant families who established businesses and created a vast web of trade routes across the Persian Gulf, Persia and the Indian sub-continent.
data.description: A prominent Persian merchant family that served as British Native Agents in 19th century Bahrain and maintained exceptionally close relationships with the ruling Al Khalifa family despite religious differences.
data.originStory: The research indicates the Al Safar were a Persian family, but does not provide specific details about their original location in Persia or the circumstances of their migration to Bahrain.

data.history: Based on the available research, the Al Dhaen family name appears in genealogical records connected to the Arabian Peninsula's tribal networks. The most documented reference shows Raisa Shehitan Al Dhaen Al Ajami as the mother of Seeta bint Fahd Al Damir, who became one of the wives of King Khalid of Saudi Arabia. This connection indicates the Al Dhaen lineage was part of the broader Ajman tribal confederation based in Al Badiyah region. Additionally, a member named Nasser Dhaen is documented as one of the founders of Al Ain Football Club in the UAE in 1968, suggesting some branches of the family established themselves in the Emirates during the mid-20th century. The presence of Sami Dhaen Al Qamzi on the Board of Directors of the UAE Central Bank indicates the family has achieved significant positions in the modern UAE financial sector.

data.history: The Al Hujairi family traces its lineage to Zubayr ibn al-Awwam (c. 594-656), one of the most prominent companions of Prophet Muhammad and a key military commander during the early Islamic conquests. Zubayr was born in Mecca to the Asad clan of the Quraysh tribe, with his father Al-Awwam ibn Khuwaylid being the brother of Khadija (Prophet Muhammad's first wife) and his mother Safiyya bint Abd al-Muttalib being Muhammad's aunt, making him both first cousin and brother-in-law to the Prophet. As one of the earliest converts to Islam at age 16, Zubayr participated in virtually all major early Islamic battles including Badr, Uhud, and the Battle of the Trench, earning the title 'Hawari Rasul Allah' (Disciple of the Messenger of God) from Muhammad himself. Following Muhammad's death, Zubayr served as a commander during the Ridda Wars under Caliph Abu Bakr and played crucial roles in the Muslim conquests of the Levant, Persia, and North Africa under Caliph Umar. He was killed in 656 during the Battle of the Camel while fighting against Caliph Ali, and his descendants, known as the Zubayrids, spread worldwide and became influential in various regions including Iraq, Iran, and the Indian subcontinent during the Umayyad campaigns in the 7th century AD.
data.description: The Al Hujairi family claims descent from Zubayr ibn al-Awwam, a prominent companion of Prophet Muhammad and early Islamic military commander whose descendants (Zubayrids) spread globally after the 7th century.
data.originStory: The family traces its lineage to Zubayr ibn al-Awwam, born around 594 CE in Mecca to the Asad clan of the Quraysh tribe. As one of the first converts to Islam and a close companion of Prophet Muhammad, Zubayr became a legendary military figure whose descendants later migrated from Arabia to various parts of the world during the early Islamic conquests.

data.history: Based on the available research, the Rafeea family appears to have Syrian origins, as evidenced by Zain Al Rafeea who was born in Daraa, Syria in 2004. The family was forced to migrate due to conflict, moving from Syria to Lebanon in 2012 during the Syrian civil war. Zain Al Rafeea grew up as a Syrian refugee in the slums of Beirut with his parents, where he was discovered for his starring role in the acclaimed 2018 Lebanese film Capernaum. Following the international success of the film, Zain received Norwegian citizenship and relocated to Norway in 2018, where he began attending school for the first time in Hammerfest. His sister Riman Al Rafeea also appears to be involved in the entertainment industry, appearing alongside him in the 2024 film The Sand Castle. The family's story reflects the broader Syrian refugee experience, with displacement leading to new opportunities in different countries.

data.history: The research provided covers three distinct individuals named Omar from different backgrounds and time periods, none of which appear to be connected to GCC family genealogy or tribal lineage. Ilhan Omar is a Somali-American politician born in 1982 in Mogadishu, whose father Nur Omar Mohamed was from the Osman Mohamud sub-clan of Majeerteen in northeastern Somalia. Her family fled Somalia during the civil war and eventually settled in Minneapolis, Minnesota. Omar bin Laden, born in 1981, is a Saudi artist and businessman, the son of Osama bin Laden and grandson of Mohammed bin Awad bin Laden, founder of the Saudi Binladin Group construction company. Mullah Omar (1959-2013) was an Afghan religious and militant leader who founded the Taliban, coming from the Tomzi clan of the Hotak tribe within the Ghilji tribal confederation of Pashtuns in Afghanistan. None of these individuals represent a unified 'Omar family' with GCC origins or connections to Gulf tribal genealogy.

data.history: Salman bin Abdulaziz Al Saud was born on 31 December 1935 as the 25th son of King Abdulaziz, the founder of Saudi Arabia, and Hassa bint Ahmed Al Sudairi, making him one of the Sudairi Seven. He served as Deputy Governor of Riyadh Province from 1954-1955, then as Governor of Riyadh Province from 1963 to 2011, overseeing the transformation of Riyadh from a mid-sized town into a major urban metropolis. In 2011, he was appointed Minister of Defense, then Crown Prince in 2012 following the death of Crown Prince Nayef. He assumed the throne on 23 January 2015 upon the death of his half-brother King Abdullah, becoming Saudi Arabia's first head of state born after the unification of Saudi Arabia. As king, his major initiatives have included the Saudi intervention in the Yemeni Civil War, Saudi Vision 2030, and various social reforms including allowing women to drive in 2017.
data.description: Salman bin Abdulaziz Al Saud has been King of Saudi Arabia since 2015, previously serving as Governor of Riyadh Province for 48 years. He is one of the Sudairi Seven and the 25th son of the kingdom's founder.
data.originStory: Born into the Al Saud ruling family, son of King Abdulaziz (founder of Saudi Arabia) and Hassa bint Ahmed Al Sudairi. Part of the Sudairi Seven - seven full brothers who form a powerful faction within the royal family.
data.modernStatus: Currently serving as King of Saudi Arabia since 2015, though his son Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman is considered the de facto ruler due to the King's reported health issues including mild vascular dementia. Mohammed replaced his father as Prime Minister in 2022.

data.history: The Al Waleed family examined here centers on Prince Al Waleed bin Talal Al Saud, born in 1955 as a grandson of Saudi Arabia's founder King Abdulaziz and Lebanon's first Prime Minister Riad Al Solh. His father Prince Talal bin Abdulaziz served as Saudi Arabia's finance minister in the early 1960s before going into exile due to his advocacy for political reform. Al Waleed's parents separated when he was seven, and he lived with his mother in Lebanon, attending schools in Beirut before completing his education in California and New York. Starting his business career in 1979 with $30,000 from his father, Al Waleed built Kingdom Holding Company into a major conglomerate with investments spanning from Citicorp to Four Seasons Hotels. His business empire expanded internationally through strategic investments in underperforming companies, making him one of the world's wealthiest individuals with a net worth reaching $39.8 billion by 2017. In November 2017, he was arrested as part of Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman's anti-corruption purge and detained at the Ritz-Carlton Riyadh for nearly three months before his release following a reported $6 billion settlement.
data.description: A branch of the Saudi royal family (Al Saud) founded by Prince Al Waleed bin Talal, who built a global business empire through Kingdom Holding Company while maintaining royal status as grandson of King Abdulaziz.
data.originStory: Al Waleed bin Talal was born in 1955 to Prince Talal bin Abdulaziz Al Saud and Mona El Solh. His paternal lineage traces to King Abdulaziz (founder of Saudi Arabia) and his Armenian grandmother Munaiyir, while his maternal lineage connects to Lebanese Prime Minister Riad Al Solh and Syrian political families.
data.modernStatus: Al Waleed remains founder, CEO and 95% owner of Kingdom Holding Company, a Saudi conglomerate with global investments in hospitality, media, and technology. He owns significant stakes in various international companies and properties including Four Seasons hotels and media companies.
data.nameEtymology: Al Waleed derives from the Arabic name Waleed, historically associated with the famous 7th-century military commander Khalid ibn al-Walid, known as 'Sayf Allah' (Sword of God).

data.history: The Mehta family represents one of India's most prominent surnames, derived from the Sanskrit word 'mahita' meaning 'great' or 'praised'. The name is found among several Indian religious groups including Hindus, Jains, Parsis, and Sikhs, distributed primarily across North and Western regions of India including Punjab, Gujarat, Maharashtra, and other states. Among different communities, Mehta serves various functions - as a Rajput clan name in Uttarakhand and Himachal Pradesh where they served as nobles and ministers under Katyuri and Chand Kings, as a title for administrative officials in Rajputana states, and as clan names among Oswal and Porwal Banias and Khatris. The research reveals specific connections to the Gulf region through Russell Mehta, managing director of Rosy Blue India, whose daughter Diya Mehta married Ayush Jatia in Bahrain in May 2017. The family has produced numerous notable figures across various fields including classical music (Zubin Mehta), literature (Gita Mehta), business, and entertainment, establishing significant cultural and economic influence both in India and internationally.
data.description: An Indian surname meaning 'great' or 'praised' found among multiple religious communities, serving as both title and clan name across various castes and regions of India.
data.originStory: Derived from the Sanskrit word 'mahita' meaning 'great' or 'praised', the Mehta surname evolved as both a title of respect for administrative officials and a hereditary surname among various Indian communities.
data.modernStatus: The Mehta name encompasses diverse modern roles including major business leadership (Russell Mehta of Rosy Blue India), international classical music direction (Zubin Mehta), literature and journalism, and entertainment industry prominence across India and internationally.

data.history: The Kumar family referenced in this research is actually the Ganguly family from Indian cinema, not a GCC family. The research provides detailed information about the Bollywood Ganguly family, originating from Bikrampur, Dhaka district, Eastern Bengal region (now Bangladesh), who settled in Khandwa, India. The family patriarch Kunjalal Ganguly was an advocate from a long line of lawyers, and his wife Gouri Rani Devi was the granddaughter of Raja Shibchandra Banerjee. The family became prominent in Hindi cinema through the three Ganguly brothers - Ashok Kumar, Anoop Kumar, and Kishore Kumar (born Abhas Kumar Ganguly) - who all became successful actors and singers in Bollywood from the 1940s through the 1980s. There is also mention of Gulshan Kumar, founder of T-Series music label, and his family including children Bhushan Kumar, Khushalii Kumar, and Tulsi Kumar, representing a different Kumar family in the entertainment industry. However, none of this research relates to any GCC (Gulf Cooperation Council) family genealogy, business history, or tribal lineage.
data.description: No GCC family information found in research - all references are to Indian cinema families.

data.history: The Goyal family name appears in multiple contexts across Indian business and political spheres, though the research provided focuses on specific individuals rather than a unified family genealogy. Naresh Goyal, born in Sangrur, Punjab in 1949, represents one prominent branch, founding Jet Airways in 1993 and becoming a major figure in Indian aviation. His family faced economic hardship when he was young, with his father dying during his childhood and their house being auctioned when he was eleven. Piyush Goyal represents another significant branch, born in Mumbai in 1964 to a politically active family - his mother Chandrakanta Goyal served as a three-time BJP MLA from Maharashtra, while his father Ved Prakash Goyal served as Union Minister for shipping and National Treasurer of the BJP for over two decades. This suggests the Goyal name encompasses multiple families across different regions of India, with notable concentrations in Punjab and Maharashtra, achieving prominence in aviation, politics, and business sectors.
data.description: The Goyal name appears across multiple prominent Indian families involved in aviation, politics, and business, with notable figures including Jet Airways founder Naresh Goyal and current Commerce Minister Piyush Goyal.
data.modernStatus: Multiple Goyal families hold significant positions in Indian business and politics, including aviation industry leadership and cabinet-level government positions

data.history: The Al Anbouri family is documented in Yemen, with members appearing in both civilian and military contexts during the early 21st century. The family suffered significant casualties during the December 17, 2009 U.S. missile strike on al-Majalah village in Abyan province, where 27 members of the extended Anbouri family were killed, including women and children. The family was among the poorest Bedouin tribes in the region, living in traditional huts and engaging in nomadic pastoral activities. One family member, Saleh Saleh Mohammed Ali al-Anbouri (known as Mohammed al-Kazemi), was identified as an AQAP commander who had been arrested in 2005 on terrorism charges, released in 2007, and subsequently moved to al-Majalah where he claimed to have renounced jihadism. In the sporting realm, Khamis Al Anbouri represented The Wave, Muscat sailing team in the 2011 Extreme Sailing Series, indicating the family's presence and integration in Omani society as well.
data.description: A family with documented presence in both Yemen and Oman, known primarily through tragic civilian casualties in the 2009 al-Majalah strike and sporting participation in Oman.
data.modernStatus: The family has documented presence in both Yemen and Oman, with members involved in various sectors including sports in Oman, though the Yemeni branch suffered significant losses in 2009.

data.history:
The Al-Shabibi family is a prominent Iraqi family from Najaf with significant contributions to Iraqi politics, education, and literature. The family's most notable figure was Sheikh Mohammed Ridha Al-Shabibi (1889-1965), who played a crucial role in Iraq's independence movement after World War I. In 1919, he served as emissary carrying petitions and messages from Iraqi political and religious figures to Sharif Hussein bin Ali and Faisal I, formally publicizing Iraq's desire for self-determination and opposition to British rule. This diplomatic effort was instrumental in the process that led to Iraq's independence on October 3, 1932.

Mohammed Ridha Al-Shabibi served multiple terms as Minister of Education (1924-1925, 1935, 1937-1938, 1941, 1948) and was president of the Chamber of Deputies from December 1943 to December 1944. He was also elected president of the Iraqi Academy in 1928-1929 and became a member of the Arabic Language Academy in Cairo in the 1930s. The family maintained its prominence through subsequent generations, with his son Sinan Al-Shabibi becoming governor of the Central Bank of Iraq in 2003 after the fall of the Baathist regime.

The family faced persecution under the Iraqi Baathist regime, with Mohammed Ridha's eldest son As'ad Al-Shabibi being abducted by the regime on November 26, 1980, for his political views. The Al-Shabibi family represents a legacy of Iraqi nationalism, educational advancement, and resistance to authoritarian rule spanning multiple generations.
data.description: A prominent Iraqi family from Najaf known for their contributions to Iraqi independence, politics, education, and literature, with particular distinction in the 20th century independence movement and modern banking.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains prominence in Iraqi society, with Sinan Al-Shabibi serving as governor of the Central Bank of Iraq from 2003. The family has produced notable figures in politics, education, and literature.

data.history: The research provides information only about Ahmed Khalfan Ghailani (born March 14, 1974), a Tanzanian citizen from Zanzibar who became involved with al-Qaeda and was convicted for his role in the 1998 U.S. embassy bombings. Ghailani was born in Zanzibar, Tanzania and served as a Muslim traveling preacher (tabligh) before joining al-Qaeda, where he became an explosives expert. He was captured in Pakistan in 2004 and later transferred to the United States for trial, ultimately receiving a life sentence in 2011. The research does not provide any information about the Al Ghailani family's origins, tribal connections, migration history, or status as a Gulf family lineage.

data.history: The name 'Al Saqlawi' appears in historical records primarily through its connection to Sheikh Rashid bin Saeed Al Maktoum's beloved horse, Saqlawi, which he would ride during evening inspections of Dubai's development projects in the 1950s and 1960s. The Saqlawi horse breed represents one of the five mythical Arabian bloodlines recognized as pure by Bedouin tribes, particularly the Ruwallah and Anizah nomadic tribes of the Arabian Desert. This lineage was historically raised by Ibn el Derre of the Ruwallah tribe and was considered among the most prized Arabian horses, with Abbas I Hilmi of Egypt reportedly paying over £3,000 in the 1850s to acquire Saklawi horses from Bedouin tribes in present-day Saudi Arabia. The connection between the Al Saqlawi family name and this prestigious horse lineage suggests a possible historical association with horse breeding or ownership of these valued animals, though the specific genealogical origins of the Al Saqlawi family are not documented in the available research.
data.description: Al Saqlawi appears to be connected to the prestigious Saklawi Arabian horse lineage, one of five pure Bedouin bloodlines. The name gained prominence through Sheikh Rashid Al Maktoum's horse Saqlawi in mid-20th century Dubai.
data.modernStatus: Current status unknown from available research

data.history:
The Al Shalan family is a prominent Arabian tribal dynasty with deep roots in the Ruwallah tribe, one of the major Anizah confederations. The family gained particular prominence under Nuri bin Hazaa Al Shalaan, who served as paramount chief of the Ruwallah. His son Nawwaf bin Nuri Al Sha'alan (1883-1921) governed Al Jawf from 1909-1919, establishing the family's political authority in the region. Nawwaf was recognized by British sources as an educated leader and advanced political thinker in the desert regions.

The family's influence extended through strategic marriages with other prominent Arabian families, including the Al Saud. Nawwaf's daughter Princess Nouf married King Abdulaziz of Saudi Arabia and had three sons: Princes Thamir, Mashhour, and Mamdouh. This marriage alliance significantly elevated the family's status within the emerging Saudi state. Princess Mashhour's daughter later married Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman, continuing the family's connection to the Saudi royal family.

In modern times, the Al Shalan family has maintained prominence across various fields. Prince Faisal Al Shalan emerged as an accomplished equestrian, representing Saudi Arabia in international competitions including the 2008 Olympics and winning gold at the 2011 Pan Arab Games. The family has also faced controversies, including Hazim al-Shaalan al-Khuzaei's tenure as Iraq's defense minister (2004-2005), which ended amid corruption allegations and his subsequent conviction for embezzlement in 2007.
data.description: The Al Shalan are a prominent Arabian family from the Ruwallah tribe of the Anizah confederation, known for their historical leadership roles and marriages into the Saudi royal family. They have maintained political and social prominence across the Gulf region.
data.originStory: The Al Shalan trace their origins to the Ruwallah tribe, part of the larger Anizah tribal confederation. They gained prominence through the leadership of Nuri bin Hazaa Al Shalaan as paramount chief of the Ruwallah.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains prominence through various channels: continued marriage ties with the Saudi royal family, representation in international equestrian sports, and involvement in regional politics, though some members have faced legal controversies.
data.nameEtymology: The name 'Sha'alan' or 'Shalan' relates to the Arabic root meaning 'to kindle' or 'ignite,' though the specific etymology is not detailed in the research.

data.history: The Al Shemmeri family is documented in Kuwait's contemporary history through the business and media activities of Ahmad Al Jabr Al Shemmeri in the early 21st century. In January 2007, Ahmad Al Jabr Al Shemmeri co-founded Alam Al Yawm newspaper alongside businessman Ahmad Al Daas, with Al Shemmeri serving as the owner of the publication. The newspaper operated as an independent and moderate publication with ties to the Kuwaiti opposition Popular Bloc, reaching a circulation of 23,000 copies by 2012. The family's prominence in Kuwait came to an abrupt end in July 2014 when the Kuwaiti government revoked the newspaper's license and stripped Ahmad Al Jabr Al Shemmeri and his family members of their Kuwaiti citizenship on July 21, 2014, following the publication's involvement in distributing controversial content regarding alleged coup plotting by former senior officials.
data.description: A Kuwaiti family known primarily through Ahmad Al Jabr Al Shemmeri's ownership of Alam Al Yawm newspaper from 2007-2014, before facing citizenship revocation by the Kuwaiti government. The family also includes athlete Fawzi Al-Shammari who competed internationally in track and field.
data.modernStatus: The family's status in Kuwait was severely impacted in 2014 when Ahmad Al Jabr Al Shemmeri and his family members had their Kuwaiti citizenship revoked following the closure of their newspaper Alam Al Yawm. The family includes notable figures in media and athletics.

data.history: Based on the available research, the Al Ahmad family appears to be a branch or lineage within the Al Sabah ruling family of Kuwait. The research shows several prominent Al Ahmad figures who are members of the House of Sabah, including Ahmad Al-Abdullah Al-Ahmad Al-Sabah (born 1952), who served as oil minister and became Prime Minister in 2024, and Ahmad Nawaf Al-Ahmad Al-Sabah (born 1956), who served as Prime Minister from 2022-2023. The Al Ahmad lineage traces back to Ahmad Al-Jaber Al-Sabah (ruled 1921-1950), the 10th ruler of Kuwait, who had multiple sons including Jaber, Sabah, Nawaf, and Mishal - all of whom became emirs of Kuwait. The research indicates this is a significant political lineage within the broader Al Sabah family structure, with members holding key governmental positions including prime ministerial roles, oil ministry, and other senior positions in Kuwait's government.
data.description: The Al Ahmad represents a prominent lineage within Kuwait's ruling Al Sabah family, tracing descent from Emir Ahmad Al-Jaber Al-Sabah (1921-1950). Members have held senior political positions including Prime Minister and various ministerial roles in modern Kuwait.
data.modernStatus: Active in Kuwait's highest political circles with family members serving as Prime Ministers, ministers, and other senior government officials. Ahmad Al-Abdullah Al-Ahmad Al-Sabah currently serves as Prime Minister of Kuwait since May 2024.

data.history: Based on the provided research, the Al Matrooshi family has produced notable figures in modern UAE society, particularly in space exploration, sports, and public service. The most prominent contemporary member is Nora Al Matrooshi, born in 1993, who became the first Emirati and Arab woman astronaut after being selected from over 4,000 applicants for the UAE Astronaut Programme's second batch. She holds a mechanical engineering degree from UAE University and worked as a piping engineer before joining NASA's 2021 Astronaut Candidate Class. Another family member, Mohammad Al Mulla, born in 1988 in Dubai, also became an astronaut in the same program, having previously served as the youngest pilot trainer in Dubai Police's Air Wing Centre. The family has also produced athletes, with Yousuf Al-Matrooshi representing the UAE in swimming at both the 2020 and 2024 Summer Olympics in the men's 100-meter freestyle. In public service, Rashid Thani Rashid Al Matrooshi currently serves as Commander-in-Chief of Dubai Civil Defence, having been appointed by Sheikh Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum through Decree No. 34 of 2025. His career spans over two decades, including serving as Director General of Dubai Civil Defence from 2003-2025, during which time the organization expanded from 8 to 26 centers and grew from 591 to 2,180 personnel.
data.description: A prominent Emirati family known for achievements in space exploration, athletics, and public service, with members serving as astronauts, Olympic athletes, and senior government officials.
data.modernStatus: The Al Matrooshi family holds significant positions in UAE's space program, civil defense, and sports sectors, with two NASA-trained astronauts and representation in Olympic competition.

data.history: The Al Mazroui family name derives from the Mazari (singular Mazrouei), an Arab tribe that forms a significant component of the Bani Yas confederation in the UAE. The Mazari are considered a subsection of the Bani Yas and formed the majority of the Bedouin component of that federation of tribes, with their traditional homeplace being the Liwa Oasis. They are regarded as the second most influential and likely the most populous section of the Bani Yas confederation, though they were originally considered to belong to a separate Ghafiri faction and traditionally followed the Hanbali school of Islamic jurisprudence before generally adopting the Maliki school in Abu Dhabi. According to the Amir of the Mazari at Adhen and Ghayl, the Mazari traditionally claim to originate from the Bani Tamim tribe, having settled in the region at an unspecified period after already embracing Islam. However, different Mazari communities hold varying views regarding their origins, with some groups identifying with the Abu Dhabi Bani Yas connection while others, particularly in Wadi Sfuni and Wadi Sfai, regard themselves as among the original inhabitants of their areas. In the nineteenth century, they contributed significant numbers of tribesmen to support Zayed bin Khalifa in conflicts against Wahhabi incursions and played important roles in securing the interior region.
data.description: The Al Mazroui are descendants of the Mazari tribe, a major Bedouin component of the Bani Yas confederation, traditionally based in Liwa Oasis and considered the second most influential section of the confederation.
data.originStory: According to the Amir of the Mazari at Adhen and Ghayl, the Mazari traditionally claim to originate from the Bani Tamim tribe and to have settled in the region at an unspecified period, having already embraced Islam prior to their arrival. However, different Mazari communities hold varying views regarding their origins.
data.tribalOrigin: Bani Yas confederation (as a subsection); claimed descent from Bani Tamim
data.modernStatus: The Mazari continue to hold prominent positions in Abu Dhabi's political and economic life. Notable current figures include Yousef bin Omar Al-Mazrui (chairman of ADNOC), Ahmad bin Mubarak Al-Mazrui (member of Abu Dhabi Executive Council), and several others in senior government and business roles. The ruler's wife is a member of the family.
data.nameEtymology: Al Mazroui derives from Mazari (the tribal name), with modern Emirati names like Mazroui being derived from tribal names or offshoots of these tribes

data.history: The Al Sowaidi family appears in contemporary Qatari records primarily through notable individuals in arts and government. Aisha Nasser Al Sowaidi emerged as a contemporary artist, creating the installation 'Central Midkhan' located in the National Museum of Qatar's VIP lounge. The piece, constructed largely from pipes and designed to spread scent in multiple directions, represents an industrial interpretation of traditional home infrastructure. Mohammed Saif Al-Sowaidi holds the position of CEO of the Qatar Investment Authority, Qatar's sovereign wealth fund, succeeding Mansoor Ebrahim Al-Mahmoud in November 2024. The family name also appears in artistic contexts through Mayasa Al Sowaidi, who won the Public Vote Prize at the 2016 Sovereign Middle East & North Africa Art Prize. Mohammed Daham Faraj Al-Suwaidi (born 1962) represented Qatar as a football defender in the 1984 Asian Cup and played his entire club career with Al-Arabi, participating in major tournaments including the 1984 Summer Olympics and 1986 FIFA World Cup qualification.
data.description: A Qatari family with notable presence in contemporary arts, finance, and sports, including the current CEO of Qatar Investment Authority and contemporary artists.
data.modernStatus: The Al Sowaidi family maintains prominent positions in Qatar's cultural and financial sectors, with Mohammed Saif Al-Sowaidi serving as CEO of the Qatar Investment Authority and family members active in Qatar's contemporary art scene.

data.history: The Al Hitmi family is a branch of the main Al Bin Ali tribe, with their presence in Qatar documented from the early 20th century. The area now known as Old Al Hitmi in Doha was first settled in the early 1900s by the Al Bin Ali tribe, and the district was initially referred to as Al Bin Ali. In the 1930s, many members of the Al Hitmi family, as a branch of the Al Bin Ali tribe, settled in this area, resulting in the district adopting its current name of Al Hitmi around the 1940s. The family name is derived from the Al-Hitmi family, which gave its name to both the Old Al Hitmi and New Al Hitmi districts in Doha. Both districts are located in the municipality of Doha, with New Al Hitmi forming Zone 37 together with Fereej Bin Omran and Hamad Medical City. The family's historical presence is primarily documented through the geographical naming of these significant districts in Qatar's capital city.
data.description: Al Hitmi is a branch of the main Al Bin Ali tribe that settled in Doha during the 1930s, giving their name to two major districts in Qatar's capital.
data.modernStatus: The family name is preserved through the Al Hitmi districts in Doha, with both Old Al Hitmi and New Al Hitmi being significant residential and commercial areas in the capital.

data.history: The Al Qu'aiti family originated from the Yafa'i tribe and rose to prominence through military service with the Nizam of Hyderabad State in India. Umar bin Awadh al-Qu'aiti became a jemadar in the Nizam's forces, and his sons leveraged this connection to establish control over Hadhramaut territories in Yemen starting in 1858. The family first took the town of Shibam from the rival Kathiris in 1858, with Awadh bin Umar (Sultan Nawaz Jang) establishing broader authority by 1867 after military campaigns supported by resources from Hyderabad. Through a series of conflicts and British-mediated agreements in the 1870s-1880s, the Qu'aitis gained control of Mukalla and its dependencies, becoming the rulers of what became known as the Qu'aiti State of Shihr and Mukalla. The family signed protectorate treaties with Britain in 1888 and 1902, with their ruler's title changed from Jemadar to Sultan and granted a 9-gun salute. The sultanate encompassed the Indian Ocean coast, the historic city of Shabwa, central Hadhramaut valleys, and controlled southern Empty Quarter territories until 1967. The monarchy ended when communist forces overran the kingdom on 17 September 1967, leading to integration into South Yemen. The current head of the household is Sultan Ghalib II bin Awadh al-Qu'aiti, who reigned briefly from October 1966 until the 1967 overthrow and now resides in Jeddah, Saudi Arabia.
data.description: The Al Qu'aiti family is a Yafa'i-origin dynasty that ruled the Qu'aiti Sultanate in Hadhramaut, Yemen from the 1860s until 1967. The family leveraged military service with the Nizam of Hyderabad to establish control over coastal and inland territories of southern Arabia.
data.originStory: The Al Qu'aiti family originated from the Yafa'i tribe. Umar bin Awadh al-Qu'aiti served as a jemadar in the forces of the Nizam of Hyderabad State in India, and his sons used this military connection and resources from Hyderabad to establish territorial control in their ancestral Hadhramaut region, beginning with the capture of Shibam from the Kathiri rivals in 1858.
data.modernStatus: The family no longer holds political power, with the sultanate ended in 1967. Current head Sultan Ghalib II bin Awadh al-Qu'aiti is a Saudi resident in Jeddah, holds degrees from Oxford and Cambridge, and has authored works on Islamic and Arab history including 'The Holy Cities, the Pilgrimage and the World of Islam' (2008).

data.history: The Al Yammahi are one of three notable sections of the Sharqiyin tribe, alongside the Hafaitat (from which the ruling family of Fujairah derives) and the Hamudiyin. The Sharqiyin were historically the dominant tribe along the East coast of the Trucial States, an area known as Shamailiyah, and were traditionally aligned with the Ghafiri faction. They settled all along the East Coast from Kalba to Dibba, as well as in the Wadi Ham and Jiri plain, and by the turn of the 20th century numbered some 7,000 strong. A 1968 census showed 90% of the tribal population of Fujairah was Sharqiyin, making them the second most numerous tribe in the Trucial States after the Bani Yas. The Al Yammahi, as part of the Sharqiyin confederation, participated in the long struggle for independence from Sharjah's suzerainty, which culminated in Fujairah's practical independence from 1901 onwards and formal British recognition as a Trucial State in 1952. Today, the Al Yammahi remain prominent in the UAE, with members holding significant political positions including representation in the Federal National Council.
data.description: The Al Yammahi are one of three major sections of the Sharqiyin tribe, historically dominant along the UAE's East coast. They are part of the tribal confederation that formed the basis of modern Fujairah emirate.
data.modernStatus: Members hold prominent political positions in the UAE, including Federal National Council representation, with Brigadier H.E Mohammed Ahmed Al Yammahi serving as a Federal National Council Member

data.history: The Al Baloushi family name is an Arabized form of the term Balochi or Baluchi, denoting Baloch ancestry from Balochistan. Many people carrying this surname trace their ancestral origins to Balochistan — a region in southwestern Pakistan and southeastern Iran. Their ancestors primarily came from the Makran coast in the 18th century, migrating to various Gulf countries including the UAE, Oman, Kuwait, Bahrain, and Qatar. The presence of Al-Balushis in Oman dates back centuries, rooted in the historical connection between Makran (present-day Pakistan) and Oman through economic and political ties, particularly via Gwadar Port which was under Omani rule for nearly two centuries until 1958. In the UAE context, the family appears in various sectors including football, with multiple players bearing the Al-Baloushi surname representing Emirati clubs and the national team. The family has also been associated with tragic historical events, as evidenced by the 1994 case of Sarah Balabagan, a Filipino domestic worker who killed her employer Almas Mohammed al-Baloushi in the UAE.
data.description: Al Baloushi (البلوشي) is a tribal surname common in Gulf countries, representing an Arabized form of Balochi, denoting Baloch ancestry from the Makran coast of Balochistan who migrated to the region primarily in the 18th century.
data.originStory: The Al Baloushi family originates from the Baloch people of Balochistan, a region spanning southwestern Pakistan and southeastern Iran. Their ancestors migrated from the Makran coast in the 18th century to various Gulf countries, with particular historical connections to Oman through the centuries-long rule of Gwadar Port.
data.modernStatus: The Al Baloushi family is distributed across Gulf countries with notable presence in sports, particularly football in the UAE, and maintains cultural and linguistic connections to their Baloch heritage while being integrated into Gulf Arab society.
data.nameEtymology: Al-Balushi is an Arabized form of the term Balochi or Baluchi, typically denoting Baloch ancestry from Balochistan

data.history: The research provided does not contain information about an "Al Saad" family as a distinct entity. Instead, it includes biographical information about several unrelated individuals with "Saad" in their names from different families and regions. Sheikh Saad Al-Abdullah Al-Salim Al-Sabah was a member of the Al-Salem branch of the ruling Al-Sabah family of Kuwait, serving as Emir briefly in 2006. Saad bin Abdulaziz Al Saud was a Saudi prince and son of King Abdulaziz, who served as governor of Asir province. These are members of established ruling dynasties (Al-Sabah and Al-Saud respectively) rather than representatives of a separate "Al Saad" family lineage.
data.description: No unified Al Saad family entity is documented in the research. The individuals mentioned are members of other established Gulf ruling families.

data.history: The Al Abdullatif family traces its origins to the Al Sheikh lineage, descendants of the 18th-century religious reformer Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab. The family's most prominent historical figure was Abdullah bin Abdullatif Al Sheikh (d. 1884-1906 era), who served as one of the principal religious teachers and advisers to the early Saudi rulers. Initially, Abdullah supported Emir Muhammad bin Abdullah Al Rashid, but later became aligned with Abdulaziz bin Abdul Rahman (the future King Abdulaziz) after the capture of Riyadh in 1902. The family solidified their position within the nascent Saudi state through strategic marriages - Abdullah's daughter Tarfa married Emir Abdulaziz in 1902, becoming the mother of future King Faisal, while his other daughters married into the Al Saud family as well. This established the Al Abdullatif branch as influential religious scholars and advisers to the Saudi royal family. The family maintained their religious authority and scholarly tradition throughout the formation and development of modern Saudi Arabia, with members continuing to serve in important religious and educational positions within the Kingdom.
data.description: The Al Abdullatif are a branch of the Al Sheikh family, descendants of Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab, who became prominent religious advisers to the early Saudi rulers through strategic marriages and scholarly influence. They are most notably the maternal family of King Faisal of Saudi Arabia.
data.originStory: The Al Abdullatif are descendants of Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab, the 18th-century religious reformer whose teachings formed the basis of the Wahhabi movement. They are part of the broader Al Sheikh family that has traditionally provided religious guidance to the Al Saud dynasty.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains their traditional role as religious scholars and advisers within Saudi Arabia's religious establishment, continuing their historical connection to the Al Saud royal family through their scholarly heritage.

data.history: The Al Bu Kharaiban is a subsection of the Na'im tribe and the ruling family from which the Rulers of the Emirate of Ajman are drawn. Al Bu Kharaiban Nuaimi rule in Ajman effectively began with Sheikh Rashid bin Humaid Al Nuaimi who, with some fifty followers, took control of the coastal settlement of Ajman from members of the Al Bu Shamis Nuaimi in a short conflict. This was sealed in 1816/17 by the taking of the fort by Rashid, who was to be a signatory of the 1820 General Maritime Treaty with the British. The Al Bu Kharaiban maintained their rule over Ajman continuously from this period, with succession passing through the family according to the ruler lists provided. During the early 20th century, Lorimer recorded some 700 Al Bu Kharaiban in Ajman town, with additional populations in Hafit village near Buraimi (140 members), and Hamriyah (90 townsfolk and 90 Bedouin). The tribe was noted for being 'warlike and predatory' in their Bedouin sections. The Al Bu Kharaiban also held influence in the Buraimi area, holding the fort in Buraimi village and serving as representatives of the Sultan, though their relationship with Abu Dhabi's Al Bu Falah was often fractious. In 1948, the Al Bu Kharaiban ruler of Ajman, Rashid bin Humaid III, took control of the village of Masfout from its Nuaimi Sheikh, incorporating it as an exclave of Ajman.
data.description: The Al Bu Kharaiban is a subsection of the Na'im tribe that has ruled the Emirate of Ajman since 1816. They are distinguished from other Na'im subsections like the Al Bu Shamis and Khawatir, and established their rule by taking Ajman from the Al Bu Shamis in a brief conflict.
data.modernStatus: The Al Bu Kharaiban continue to rule the Emirate of Ajman as part of the United Arab Emirates, with Sheikh Humaid bin Rashid Al Nuaimi III serving as the current ruler since 1981. Ajman joined the UAE federation in 1971 under Sheikh Rashid bin Humayd Al Nuaimi.

data.history: The Al Orair (آل العرير) family traces its origins to the Bani Khalid tribe, which ruled Eastern Arabia for an extended period and was the most powerful tribe in the region during the late 18th century. The family's most significant historical figure was Wadha bint Muhammad Al Orair, daughter of Muhammad Al Orair and granddaughter of the chief of the Bani Khalid tribe who ruled Al Hasa. In 1896, Wadha married Abdulaziz bin Abdul Rahman Al Saud (Ibn Saud) in Kuwait during the Al Saud family's exile period. This marriage alliance was politically significant as it connected the future founder of Saudi Arabia to one of the most influential tribal families of Eastern Arabia. Wadha became the mother of several prominent Saudi princes, including the future King Saud of Saudi Arabia (born 1902), Prince Turki I (the original crown prince who died in 1919), and other members of the Saudi royal family. The Al Orair connection to the Bani Khalid tribe positioned them as part of the broader tribal confederation that had historically controlled the eastern regions of the Arabian Peninsula, including Al-Qatif and surrounding areas where the family had been established.
data.description: The Al Orair family originates from the powerful Bani Khalid tribe that ruled Eastern Arabia, most notably represented by Wadha bint Muhammad Al Orair who married Saudi Arabia's founder Ibn Saud in 1896 and became mother to King Saud and other prominent Saudi princes.
data.originStory: The Al Orair family belonged to the Bani Khalid tribe, whose chief ruled the Al Hasa region of Eastern Arabia. Muhammad Al Orair was a prominent member of this tribe, and his daughter Wadha bint Muhammad Al Orair represented a significant tribal alliance when she married Ibn Saud during the Al Saud family's exile in Kuwait.
data.modernStatus: The Al Orair family's modern significance derives from their historical marriage alliance with the Al Saud royal family through Wadha bint Muhammad Al Orair, making them maternal ancestors of King Saud and other Saudi royal family members.

data.history: The Al Jalahmah (also spelled Al Jalahma) were a prominent Arab tribal family that formed part of the larger Bani Utbah confederation. According to historical records, they migrated from their ancestral homeland in Najd during a prolonged drought in the 17th century, traveling through Al Aflaj, Qatif, and Al Ahsa before eventually reaching Freiha in northwestern Qatar. Around 1766, the Al Jalahmah, along with the Al Khalifa clan, migrated from Kuwait to Zubarah in Qatar, where they initially cooperated in establishing a successful trading port. However, tensions arose when the Al Khalifa refused to share the economic gains from trade ventures with the Al Jalahmah, leading to the latter's relocation to Al Ruwais and later to Khor Hassan in northwest Qatar. Despite their grievances, the Al Jalahmah agreed to fight alongside the Al Khalifa in the 1783 battle that drove the Persians from Bahrain. After Bahrain was annexed by the Al Khalifa, the Al Jalahmah felt dissatisfied with their share of the rewards and adopted piracy as their primary livelihood under the leadership of Rahmah ibn Jabir al-Jalhami. The family's most famous figure, Rahmah ibn Jabir, became one of the most notorious pirates in the Persian Gulf, establishing his base at Khor Hassan and later Dammam, from where he conducted raids against the Al Khalifa and other regional powers until his death in 1826.
data.description: The Al Jalahmah were a prominent Arab clan within the Bani Utbah confederation who migrated from Najd to Qatar in the 18th century. They are most famous for producing Rahmah ibn Jabir al-Jalhami, one of the Persian Gulf's most notorious pirates.
data.originStory: The Al Jalahmah originated in the Najd region of central Arabia and were part of the Anaza tribe. During a prolonged drought in the 17th century, they joined other Utub clans in migrating eastward, eventually reaching Kuwait before moving to Qatar around 1766.

data.history: The Al Zayed family, more accurately known as the Al Nahyan family, traces its origins to Sheikh Zayed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan ('Zayed the Great') who ruled Abu Dhabi from 1855 to 1909. The family became the ruling dynasty of Abu Dhabi when Sheikh Sultan bin Khalifa Al Nahyan ruled from 1922 until his death in 1926. His youngest son, Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan, was born in 1918 at Qasr Al Hosn, Abu Dhabi, and moved to Al Ain in 1926 after his father's death. Zayed bin Sultan replaced his older brother Sheikh Shakhbut bin Sultan as ruler of Abu Dhabi on 6 August 1966 through a bloodless coup supported by the ruling family and the British, due to frustrations over Shakhbut's reluctance to spend oil revenues on development. Following the British withdrawal announcement in 1968, Sheikh Zayed became a founding father of the United Arab Emirates in 1971, serving as its first president until his death in 2004. The family has maintained continuous rule over Abu Dhabi, with Zayed's eldest son Khalifa bin Zayed serving as the second president from 2004 to 2022, followed by his half-brother Mohamed bin Zayed who became the third president. On 29 March 2023, Mohamed appointed his eldest son Khaled bin Mohamed as Crown Prince of Abu Dhabi, continuing the family's dynastic succession.
data.description: The Al Nahyan family is the ruling dynasty of Abu Dhabi and has provided three presidents of the United Arab Emirates since the federation's founding in 1971. They transformed from traditional Bedouin rulers into leaders of a modern oil-rich state and global financial power.
data.originStory: The family's documented political prominence begins with Sheikh Zayed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan ('Zayed the Great') who ruled Abu Dhabi from 1855 to 1909. The current ruling line descends from his son Sultan bin Khalifa Al Nahyan, whose youngest son Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan became the founding president of the UAE.
data.modernStatus: The Al Nahyan family currently rules Abu Dhabi and the UAE, with Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan serving as the third president since 2022. They control massive sovereign wealth funds including the Abu Dhabi Investment Authority (nearly $1 trillion in assets) and ADNOC, and have established major cultural institutions like Louvre Abu Dhabi and NYU Abu Dhabi.
data.nameEtymology: The current ruler Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan was named after his grandfather, Sheikh Zayed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan, known as 'Zayed the Great' who ruled from 1855 to 1909.

data.history: The research provided does not contain information about an Al Khamis family as a distinct GCC genealogical entity. While the name 'Khamis' appears in various contexts throughout the research - including Khamis Gaddafi (Libyan political figure), Khamis Al-Owairan (Saudi footballer), and references to Al Khamis family members in UAE royal marriages - there is no coherent narrative about a specific Al Khamis family's origins, migration, or establishment in the Gulf region. The research mentions that Sheikh Khalifa bin Zayed Al Nahyan's wife Shamsa bint Suhail Al Mazrouei was 'from a famous Al Khamis family of the Al Shikr branch of the Mazaria tribe in Liwa,' but provides no further details about this family's history or status. Additionally, the city of Khamis Mushayt in Saudi Arabia appears in the research but is described as being named after 'a man called Mushayt' rather than connected to any Al Khamis family lineage.
data.description: Research does not provide sufficient information to establish the existence or characteristics of a distinct Al Khamis family in GCC genealogical context.

data.history: The Al Shihhi family traces its origins to the Shihuh tribe, an Arab tribe inhabiting the northern part of the Hajar Mountain range, specifically in the Ruus Al Jibal (Musandam Peninsula) region spanning both the UAE and Oman. The Shihuh tribe has been documented as having long maintained influence over both east and west coast settlements of the northern UAE and Oman, fiercely preserving their identity and independence. At the turn of the 20th century, the tribe numbered approximately 21,500 people, with about 14,500 settled along the coast and 7,000 in the mountainous interior. The tribe follows seasonal migration patterns, moving between mountain communities during winter months for agriculture and coastal areas during warmer months for date harvesting and pearl fishing. Research indicates the tribe incorporates elements of Iranian origin, with the Arab majority believed linked to Yemen immigration waves bringing Malik bin Fahm immigrants north in the second century. The Shihuh have historically been difficult to govern due to their inaccessible mountain terrain, frequently conflicting with central authorities while maintaining complex relationships with various regional powers including the Al Qasimi of Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah, and showing greater acceptance of Muscat's suzerainty.
data.description: The Al Shihhi family originates from the Shihuh tribe, an Arab mountain tribe of the Musandam Peninsula known for their seasonal migration between mountains and coast, distinctive culture, and historical independence. The tribe has maintained influence across northern UAE and Oman settlements while preserving their unique identity and dialect.
data.originStory: The Arab element of the Shihuh tribe's make-up is thought to be linked to the wave of immigration from Yemen, which brought groups of Malik bin Fahm immigrants north in the second century. The tribe incorporates elements of Iranian origin, with some subsections speaking Iranian languages, leading to their distinctive cultural identity separate from desert Bedouin traditions.
data.modernStatus: Members of the Al Shihhi family continue to inhabit the UAE and Oman, with the name Al Shehhi being a common family name in both countries today. The family maintains connections to their ancestral lands in the Musandam Peninsula and Ras Al Khaimah areas.

folkLegends.title: Non-Arab Origin Theory
folkLegends.story: Some traditions claimed the Shihuh were of Portuguese extraction due to their lifestyle and distinctive dialect being markedly different from desert Bedouin, leading to speculation about non-Arab descent.

data.nameEtymology: In the singular, the tribal name becomes Al Shehhi, derived from the Arabic al-Shiḥuḥ (الشحوح), which is the plural form of the tribal designation.

data.history: The Al Muhanna family name appears in multiple historical contexts across the Arabian Peninsula, though the research does not establish a unified genealogical connection between all bearers of this name. The most prominent historical figure was Al-Muhanna bin Jayfar, an Ibadi scholar who served as the sixth imam of the Imamate of Oman from April 841 until his death in October 851. He hailed from the Yahmad clan of Azd and ruled from Nizwa, overseeing significant military reorganization including expansion of the navy to 300 warships and establishment of a 10,000-strong standing army. His reign was marked by economic growth through improvements to aflaj irrigation systems and population increases, with one district of Nizwa housing up to 14,000 inhabitants. However, his increasingly centralized governance created opposition from traditional Ibadi ulama who felt he disregarded the traditional power-sharing structure of the imamate. In modern times, the name Al Muhanna appears in various Gulf contexts, including Thabit Al Muhanna who became governor of Kuwait's Capital Governorate in 2014, and Haifa Al Muhanna who was mentioned as one of King Abdullah of Saudi Arabia's wives and mother of his youngest son Prince Bandar.
data.description: Al Muhanna is a family name with historical roots in Oman's Ibadi imamate and modern presence across Gulf states. The name is associated with both medieval Islamic scholarship and contemporary Gulf political and business circles.

data.modernStatus: Contemporary Al Muhanna family members hold positions in Gulf government and business, including governorship roles in Kuwait

data.history: The Al Dhanhani family appears in the research as a contemporary Emirati family with documented presence in the UAE. The most prominent reference is to Ahmed Al Dhanhani, who served as Attorney General for the State Security Prosecution and announced the execution of Alaa Bader Abdullah (the convicted murderer of Ibolya Ryan) on July 13, 2015, following approval from President Khalifa bin Zayed Al Nahyan. Additionally, there is a reference to Fahad Al-Dhanhani as a footballer, and Rehaab Al Dhanhani who created content about Al Muhtarqah, a mountainous area in Fujairah emirate. The research also mentions Al Muhtarqah was historically populated mainly by the Sharqiyin tribe, a tribal section of Jamāmaḩah, though no direct connection to the Al Dhanhani family is established in the available sources.

data.history: The Al Jadgal (also known as Jadgal) are an Indo-Aryan ethno-linguistic group originally from the lower Indus Valley region of Sindh and Balochistan. The Jadgal people speak Jadgali, an Indo-Aryan language most closely related to Sindhi and Lasi. According to historical accounts, they left their homeland after a defeat from the ruler of Sindh and moved westward during the reign of Shah Abbas, eventually settling in the Dashtyari region of Iran's Sistan and Balochistan Province. The majority of Jadgal populations are found in Pakistan (estimated 15,600 in 2004) and Iran (at least 25,000 in 2008), with immigrant communities established in Oman and the United Arab Emirates where they are known as az-zighālī or az-zijālī. In Iran, they are concentrated in areas like Pullān, Pīr Suhrāb and Bāhū Kalāt, while maintaining their distinct cultural identity despite being surrounded by Balochi-speaking communities. Some Jadgal claim Baloch origin and report having changed their language due to interactions with neighbors when they were settled in Las Bela region at the eastern end of Balochistan.
data.description: The Al Jadgal are an Indo-Aryan ethno-linguistic group from Pakistan, Iran, Oman and UAE, speaking Jadgali language and historically originating from the Sindh-Balochistan region. They are considered among the ethnic minorities of Pakistan and are related to early Sindhi peoples of Makran.
data.originStory: According to historical accounts, the Jadgal left their original homeland after suffering a defeat from the ruler of Sindh. They then moved westward and eventually settled in the Dashtyari region of Iran during the reign of Shah Abbas. Some Jadgal claim to be of Baloch origin and report having changed their language because of interactions with their neighbors when they were settled in Las Bela region.
data.modernStatus: The Jadgal maintain communities across Pakistan (15,600), Iran (25,000+), Oman, and UAE. In Iran, they practice a vital language culture with positive attitudes toward Jadgali, though Balochi serves as the language of wider communication. They are considered among Pakistan's ethnic minorities and are found scattered across Balochistan province.
data.nameEtymology: The term Jadgal is of Balochi origin, but is now used by the Jadgal themselves alongside their earlier endonym Nummaṛ, which is the source of the language names Nummaṛī and Nummaṛikī. In Balochi language, traditional terms for Sindhis include Jadgal and Jamote, derived from the prefix Jatt referring to the tribe and suffix gal meaning 'speech'.

migrationPath.description: Historical migration from Sindh after defeat by ruler of Sindh, settling in Las Bela region
Westward migration during reign of Shah Abbas, settling in Dashtyari region of Iranian Balochistan

connections.context: Jadgals are related to early Sindhi peoples of Makran region and speak a language closely related to Sindhi

data.history: The Al Zehri family appears to be primarily associated with the Baloch Zehri tribe based in Balochistan, Pakistan. The research shows Sardar Sanaullah Khan Zehri as the Sardar of the Zehri tribe and chief of Jhalawan, with his father Sardar Doda Khan Zehri being a tribal leader who supported the Pakistan Movement. The family has its base in the Khuzdar District of Balochistan, specifically in Anjeera where Sanaullah Khan Zehri was born. The Zehri tribe is mentioned in the context of the Balochistan Liberation Army's activities, with the BLA claiming responsibility for a 2013 attack that killed Sanaullah's son, brother, and nephew. However, the research does not provide comprehensive historical information about the broader Al Zehri family origins, migration patterns, or connection to GCC countries. The available information focuses primarily on the Pakistani Baloch branch of the family and their political involvement in Balochistan province.
data.description: Al Zehri appears to be associated with the Baloch Zehri tribe of Balochistan, Pakistan, with documented political leadership roles. Limited information available about broader family history or GCC connections.

data.history: The Al Jamali family traces their origins to Bani Shaiba and have served as hereditary custodians of the Al-Kadhimiya Mosque in Baghdad since 1611, when Ottoman Sultan Ahmed I granted Jamal al-Din bin Mullah Ali, their ancestor from whom the family takes its name, a royal firman (decree) for full custodianship of the mosque and shrine. The family maintained this sacred responsibility for centuries, with Sheikh Abbas al-Jamali living next to the mosque where visitors would consult him on religious matters. The Al Jamali family produced one of Iraq's most distinguished statesmen in Muhammad Fadhel al-Jamali (1903-1997), who became a prominent educator, diplomat, and politician during Iraq's royal era. He served twice as Prime Minister of Iraq and played a crucial role in founding the United Nations, signing the UN Charter on behalf of Iraq in 1945. After the 1958 revolution, he was imprisoned and later exiled to Tunisia, where he served as an advisor to President Habib Bourguiba until his death in 1997.
data.description: The Al Jamali family are hereditary custodians of Baghdad's Al-Kadhimiya Mosque since 1611, descended from Bani Shaiba, most notably producing Iraqi statesman and UN co-founder Muhammad Fadhel al-Jamali.
data.originStory: The family originates from Bani Shaiba and became known as Al Jamali after their ancestor Jamal al-Din bin Mullah Ali, who was granted custodianship of Al-Kadhimiya Mosque by Ottoman Sultan Ahmed I in 1611 through a royal firman issued in Muharram 1020 AH (1611 AD).
data.modernStatus: The family maintains their historical religious custodial role at Al-Kadhimiya Mosque in Baghdad, with their most famous member Muhammad Fadhel al-Jamali having established a lasting legacy in international diplomacy and education.
data.nameEtymology: The family name derives from their ancestor Jamal al-Din bin Mullah Ali (Jamal ed-Din bin Mullah Ali), from whom they get their name 'al-Jamali'

data.history: The Al Magsi family is a Baloch tribal family with documented presence in the Balochistan region of present-day Pakistan. The most prominent documented figure from this family is Nawab Mir Yousaf Aziz Magsi (1908-1935), who served as a significant Baloch leader and co-founded the Anjuman-e-Ittehad-e-Balochan-wa-Balochistan alongside Abdul Aziz Kurd. This organization appears to have been an early political movement focused on Baloch unity and rights in the region that would later become Pakistan. The family's leadership role is evidenced by Yousaf Aziz Magsi's title of Nawab, indicating noble or leadership status within the traditional Baloch tribal structure. His contributions to Baloch political organization were considered significant enough that in 2011, a commemorative seminar was held at the National Language Authority in Islamabad to honor his legacy. The Al Magsi family represents one of the notable Baloch tribal families that played a role in the political development of Balochistan during the early to mid-20th century, particularly in the period leading up to and following Pakistan's independence in 1947.
data.description: The Al Magsi is a Baloch tribal family from the Balochistan region, most notably represented by Nawab Mir Yousaf Aziz Magsi (1908-1935), who was a prominent Baloch leader and co-founder of an early Baloch unity organization. The family has been recognized for its contributions to Baloch political movements in the early 20th century.
data.originStory: The research indicates the Al Magsi family is of Baloch origin, but specific details about their founding story or migration to their current territories are not provided in the available sources.
data.modernStatus: No specific information about the current business or political status of the Al Magsi family is provided in the research.
