data.history: The Al Narui (also spelled Nahrui, Narui, or Naurui) are a Baloch tribe with a documented presence in the western regions of Balochistan dating to the late 19th century. According to historical records, in 1897 the western regions of Balochistan were under the leadership of the chieftains of the Narui tribe, indicating their significant political authority in the area. The tribe appears to have been part of the broader Baloch tribal confederation system that characterized the region's governance structure. During the late 19th and early 20th centuries, the Narui were involved in resistance movements against Persian rule, with Sardar Hussein Narui Baloch leading an uprising against Persia at the end of the 19th century, which was ultimately crushed by joint Anglo-Persian mission forces. Historical records also mention Sardar Sharif Nahrui as part of a union of Baloch tribes under the leadership of Sardar Ebrahim Khan Sanjarani in the Sistan region, where they ruled with their capital at Chakansur in the early and late 19th century before being defeated by Afghan forces under Abdur Rahman Khan in 1882.
data.description: The Al Narui are a Baloch tribe that held chieftain authority in western Balochistan in the late 19th century and participated in resistance movements against Persian rule.
data.originStory: The Al Narui are part of the broader Baloch ethnic group, which according to historical sources may trace their ultimate origins to the Median peoples of ancient Iran, with migrations from the Caspian region eastward toward Balochistan occurring over several centuries during the late Sasanian period.

data.history: Based on the available research, the Al Rasheedi family name appears in multiple contexts across the Gulf region, but no comprehensive genealogical or tribal history is provided in the sources. The research shows individuals with the Al Rasheedi surname in various fields - Saad al-Khanfour al-Rasheedi serves as a Kuwaiti politician representing the fourth district in the National Assembly since 2008, having worked previously at the Interior Ministry. The name also appears in academic contexts, with mentions of an 'Ilyas Rasheedi Gold medal' awarded to Pakistani actor Mohammad Ali in 1998, suggesting possible connections to literary or cultural circles. data.history: The Al Dosary family research primarily documents Ghanem Humood al-Masarir al-Dosari, a prominent Saudi human rights activist and political satirist born in 1980 in Aflaj, Saudi Arabia. He left his hometown of Al-Kharj in 2003 to study computer science at the University of Portsmouth in the UK, later establishing himself as a vocal critic of the Saudi government through his London-based YouTube show 'The Ghanem Show.' His full name structure 'al-Masarir al-Dosari' suggests the al-Masarir lineage within the broader Al Dosary tribal confederation. The family name appears in various regional contexts, including connections to the Riyadh International Book Fair where Al Dosary publications and authors have participated. The research indicates the family has roots in the Aflaj and Al-Kharj regions of Saudi Arabia, with some members achieving prominence in media, activism, and intellectual circles.
data.description: Al Dosary is a Saudi Arabian family with documented presence in the Aflaj and Al-Kharj regions, known for producing prominent figures in human rights activism, media, and intellectual discourse.
data.modernStatus: The family includes notable contemporary figures such as Ghanem al-Dosari, a London-based human rights activist and political satirist who has gained international attention for his criticism of the Saudi government. Family members have also participated in Saudi Arabia's intellectual and literary scene through events like the Riyadh International Book Fair.

data.history: The Al Asiri family name is derived from the Asir Province of Saudi Arabia, as confirmed by the Wikipedia entry on Abdullah al-Asiri which states 'His name is an ascription to the Asir Province of Saudi Arabia.' The family gained international notoriety through Ibrahim Hassan Tali al-Asiri and his brother Abdullah al-Asiri, both born into what was described as 'a religious and military family in Riyadh with four brothers and three sisters.' Their father was identified as a retired soldier. The family became prominently known in the context of terrorism when both brothers joined al-Qaeda in the Arabian Peninsula, with Ibrahim becoming the organization's chief bomb-maker and Abdullah carrying out a suicide bombing attempt against Saudi Deputy Minister of Interior Prince Mohammed bin Nayef in 2009. The Al Asiri name also appears in other contexts, with Major General Ahmad Hassan Mohammad Asiri serving as a high-ranking Saudi military officer and former spokesman for the Saudi-led coalition in Yemen, though no family relationship to the terrorist brothers is established in the available research.
data.description: Al Asiri is a Saudi family name indicating origin from the Asir Province, known primarily through members involved in terrorism and military service.
data.originStory: The family originates from the Asir Province of Saudi Arabia, with the name serving as a geographical ascription to this region.
data.modernStatus: The family has produced both notorious terrorists and high-ranking military officials, with Ibrahim al-Asiri becoming al-Qaeda's chief bomb-maker before his death in 2017, and Ahmad Asiri serving as a Major General and former coalition spokesman.
data.nameEtymology: Al-Asiri derives from Asir Province in Saudi Arabia, functioning as a geographical ascription.

data.history:
The Al ash-Sheikh (آل الشيخ) are Saudi Arabia's leading religious family, descended from Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab (1703-1792), the 18th century founder of the Wahhabi sect. Ibn Abd al-Wahhab was born in Nejd and became influenced by the teachings of Ibn Taymiya, preaching a puritanical form of Islam. In the 1740s, he moved to Diriyah where he formed a crucial alliance with Muhammad ibn Saud, founder of the Al Saud dynasty. The formal agreement concluded in 1744 established a power-sharing arrangement: the Al Saud would maintain political authority while the Al ash-Sheikh would lead religious matters. This pact has remained in place for nearly 300 years, with the Al ash-Sheikh using their religious-moral authority to legitimize Al Saud rule in exchange for maintaining their religious authority and upholding Wahhabi doctrine.

The family faced severe setbacks in 1818 when Ottoman forces destroyed the First Saudi State, executing many Al ash-Sheikh members including Sulayman ibn Abd Allah, a grandson of Ibn Abd al-Wahhab. An entire branch was exiled to Egypt and never returned, significantly weakening the religious establishment. During the 19th century, the family's fortunes declined as the Al Saud struggled with rivals like the Al Rashid of Ha'il. When the Al Saud were defeated in 1891, religious leader Abd Allah bin Abd al-Latif initially sided with the Al-Rashid but returned to the Al Saud when Abdul Aziz re-established Saudi control in 1902.

In modern Saudi Arabia, the Al ash-Sheikh remain the kingdom's leading religious family, second in prestige only to the royal family. They have historically dominated key religious institutions like the Senior Council of Ulema and traditionally held the office of Grand Mufti. The families are closely linked through extensive intermarriage, with King Faisal's mother being Tarfa bint Abdullah Al ash-Sheikh. While their dominance has somewhat diminished in recent decades due to an influx of other families into religious seminaries, they continue to hold important religious and government positions, including current ministers and the former Grand Mufti Abdul-Aziz ibn Abdullah Al ash-Sheikh who served until 2025.
data.description: The Al ash-Sheikh are Saudi Arabia's leading religious family, descendants of Wahhabi founder Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab, who formed a crucial 300-year power-sharing alliance with the Al Saud royal family in 1744. They maintain religious authority while legitimizing Al Saud political rule through their religious-moral authority.
data.originStory: The family originates from Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab al-Tamimi al-Najdi (1703-1792), an Islamic religious reformer born in Nejd who founded the Wahhabi sect. He moved to Diriyah in the 1740s where he formed an alliance with Muhammad ibn Saud, establishing the religious-political partnership that continues today.
data.modernStatus: Leading religious family in Saudi Arabia, holding key religious positions including former Grand Mufti (until 2025), current Minister of Islamic Affairs (Saleh bin Abdul-Aziz Al ash-Sheikh), Minister of Municipal and Rural Affairs (Abdullatif bin Abdulmalik bin Omar Al-ash Sheikh), and Minister of Entertainment (Turki Al-Sheikh). They maintain religious authority through the 300-year pact with Al Saud.
data.nameEtymology: Al ash-Sheikh (آل الشيخ) translates as 'family of the Sheikh' or 'House of the Sheikh'. The term ash-Sheikh refers to Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab, who was known as 'the Sheikh', a term of respect for a noted elder, teacher or religious leader.

data.history: Al Baik is a Saudi Arabian fast food restaurant chain that was founded in 1974 in Jeddah. According to the research, Al Baik began as a local fast-food establishment specializing in broasted (broiled and roasted) chicken, commonly known by locals as 'Broast,' along with a variety of seafood offerings. The chain established itself as the most popular local fast-food brand in the Hejaz region, with branches initially concentrated in Jeddah and the neighboring cities of Makkah, Madinah, and Yanbu. The business has maintained its regional focus for decades, becoming deeply embedded in the local food culture of western Saudi Arabia. In recent years, Al Baik has begun international expansion, opening branches in Bahrain and Dubai in the UAE, marking its first ventures outside Saudi Arabia.
data.description: Al Baik is a Saudi Arabian fast food chain founded in 1974 in Jeddah, famous for its broasted chicken and seafood, and considered the most popular local fast-food brand in the region.
data.modernStatus: Popular fast food restaurant chain with locations in Jeddah, Makkah, Madinah, Yanbu, and recent international expansion to Bahrain and Dubai

timelineEvents.description: Al Baik fast food chain established in Jeddah

data.history: The Seddiqi family is documented in the research through several notable figures across different fields. The most prominent religious figure is Kazem Rajabi Seddiqi, born March 4, 1951, who became Tehran's Friday Prayer Temporary Imam in August 2009, appointed by Ayatollah Khamenei. He studied at the Haqqani Seminary in Qom starting in 1963 and became a Shia scholar and Mujtahid. The family name also appears in the business sector through Ahmed Seddiqi & Sons, which partnered with HAMG in acquiring Al Manara International Jewellery, one of the UAE's major retailers of luxury watches and jewelry. Additionally, the prominent Seddiqi family was among the co-founders of Swiss International Scientific School in Dubai (SISD), established in 2012, backing founder Omar Danial's educational venture in Dubai Healthcare City.
data.description: The Seddiqi family includes prominent religious and business figures, notably Tehran's Friday Prayer leader Kazem Rajabi Seddiqi and business interests in luxury retail and education in the UAE.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains significant religious influence through Kazem Seddiqi's role as Tehran's Friday Prayer Imam, and substantial business presence in the UAE through Ahmed Seddiqi & Sons luxury retail operations and co-founding Swiss International Scientific School in Dubai.

data.history: The Attar name derives from the Arabic word meaning apothecary, pharmacist, spice dealer, or perfumer, referring to an occupational designation rather than a specific tribal lineage. The most famous historical bearer of this name was Faridoddin Abu Hamed Mohammad Attar Nishapuri (c. 1145 – c. 1221), a Persian Sufi poet and mystic from Nishapur in medieval Khorasan (northeastern Iran). Attar was born to a Persian family and practiced as a pharmacist, attending to customers who would confide their troubles to him, which deeply affected his spiritual development. He eventually abandoned his pharmacy and traveled extensively throughout the Islamic world - to Baghdad, Basra, Kufa, Mecca, Medina, Damascus, Khwarizm, Turkistan, and India - meeting with Sufi masters and developing his mystical philosophy. He died violently at age 78 during the Mongol massacre of Nishapur in April 1221. In the modern era, the Attar name appears across the Middle East and beyond, with notable figures including Saudi Arabian writer Ahmad Abd al-Ghafur Attar (1916–1991), Lebanese footballer Ali Attar, and Iraqi-American sports manager Audie Attar, though these appear to be separate family lines rather than descendants of a single origin.
data.description: Attar is an occupational surname meaning apothecary or perfumer in Arabic, most famously associated with the 12th-century Persian Sufi poet Attar of Nishapur, with modern bearers found across various Middle Eastern countries.
data.originStory: The name Attar originates from the Arabic noun for perfume or fragrance, with an occupational meaning referring to apothecaries, pharmacists, spice dealers, or perfumers. The most historically significant bearer was the Persian mystic poet from Nishapur who took this as a pen-name reflecting his profession.
data.modernStatus: Contemporary Attar families appear across the Middle East in various professions, including journalism, sports, business, and academia, though they do not appear to represent a unified family structure.
data.nameEtymology: Derived from Arabic 'attar' meaning apothecary, pharmacist, spice dealer, or perfumer, related to the Arabic noun for perfume or fragrance. There is an exaggerated form 'Atir' or 'Ater' meaning 'perfumed, sweet-smelling, aromatic, fragrant'.

data.history: The Bindawood family's documented business history begins in 1984 when they opened their first BinDawood store in Mecca, Saudi Arabia. The family established themselves as retail entrepreneurs, building what would become one of Saudi Arabia's major supermarket and hypermarket chains. By 2001, they had expanded significantly enough to acquire Danube Company Limited, which had originally opened its first hypermarket in Jeddah in 1993. Under Bindawood ownership, Danube grew to 47 locations across the Kingdom. The family continued to modernize their operations, launching Danube Online in 2017 as an early mover in Saudi Arabia's eCommerce grocery space, followed by the BinDawood App in 2019. BinDawood Holding went public on the Saudi Stock Exchange (Tadawul) on October 21, 2020, trading under symbol 4161. By May 2022, the family's retail empire had grown to encompass 78 stores total - 52 hypermarkets and 26 supermarkets spread throughout Saudi Arabia, with operations in major cities including Mecca, Medina, Jeddah, Riyadh, Al Khobar, Dammam, and Taif.
data.description: The Bindawood family are Saudi retail entrepreneurs who built one of the Kingdom's major supermarket chains, starting with a single store in Mecca in 1984 and growing to a publicly-traded company with 78 locations by 2022. They are pioneers in Saudi Arabia's grocery eCommerce sector.
data.modernStatus: BinDawood Holding is a publicly-traded Saudi retail company listed on Tadawul since October 2020. As of 2022, they operate 78 stores (52 hypermarkets, 26 supermarkets) across Saudi Arabia under the BinDawood and Danube brands. In 2021, the company posted revenues of SR 4.3 billion with profits of SR 240 million. They have expanded internationally, acquiring 80.5% of French company Ykone in 2022 through their subsidiary Future Retail for Information Technology Co.

data.history: The Al Shaibi (Bani Shaiba) are an Arab clan belonging to the Banu Abd al-Dar sub-clan of the Quraysh tribal confederation in the Hejaz region of Saudi Arabia. They are primarily descendants of Uthman ibn Talha, his father Talha ibn Abdullah, and his grandfather Abd al-Dar ibn Qusai. The family has held the hereditary role of Custodian of the Kaaba and keeper of its keys since the pre-Islamic period, a responsibility that was formally confirmed by Prophet Muhammad during the Conquest of Mecca in 630 CE. According to Islamic tradition, Muhammad handed the keys to Uthman ibn Talha after initially having them taken by Ali ibn Abi Talib, but then receiving a revelation to return them to their rightful custodian. Muhammad declared: 'Take it, O Bani Talha, eternally up to the Day of Resurrection, and it will not be taken from you unless by an unjust, oppressive tyrant.' The custodianship has continued uninterrupted through their descendants for over 1,400 years. The current custodian is Abdul Wahab bin Zain Al-Abidin Al-Shaibi, who assumed the role in June 2024 following the death of Dr. Saleh bin Zain Al-Abidin Al-Shaibi. The family's responsibilities include managing the Kaaba, conducting cleaning ceremonies, replacing the kiswah (covering), and safeguarding the keys to the sanctuary.
data.description: The Al Shaibi are the hereditary custodians of the Kaaba in Mecca, holding the keys to Islam's holiest site since pre-Islamic times. They are descendants of the Quraysh tribe's Banu Abd al-Dar sub-clan and have maintained this sacred responsibility for over 1,400 years.
data.originStory: The keys to the Kaaba were originally held by various tribes including Tasm and Khuza'a before passing to Qusai ibn Kilab of the Quraysh, who gave them to his son Abd al-Dar. The keys eventually came to Shaiba, from whom the Bani Shaiba derive their name. During the Conquest of Mecca in 630 CE, Prophet Muhammad confirmed their custodianship after initially having the keys taken by Ali ibn Abi Talib, but then receiving a revelation to return them to Uthman ibn Talha of the Bani Shaiba.
data.modernStatus: The Al Shaibi family continues to serve as the official custodians of the Kaaba under the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia, maintaining their hereditary role in the management of Islam's holiest site and conducting religious ceremonies.
data.nameEtymology: The name 'Bani Shaiba' means 'the sons of Shaiba,' referring to their ancestor Shaiba ibn Uthman who held the keys to the Kaaba.

timelineEvents.title: Confirmation of Custodianship
timelineEvents.description: Prophet Muhammad confirms the Al Shaibi family's hereditary custodianship of the Kaaba during the Conquest of Mecca

data.history: The Al Masri family encompasses multiple distinct lineages across the Middle East, united primarily by the surname meaning 'Egyptian' in Arabic. The most prominent branch includes Palestinian businessman Munib al-Masri (born 1934), known as the 'Duke of Nablus' and 'Palestinian Rothschild,' who made his fortune in the oil industry before returning to establish a stock exchange in Nablus in the 1990s. His nephew Bashar Masri founded Rawabi, the West Bank's first planned city, while his cousin Sabih al-Masri became chairman of Arab Bank. Another significant figure was Abu Ayyub al-Masri (1967-2010), born Abdel Moneim Ezz El-Din Ali Al-Badawi in Egypt, who became a leader of Al-Qaeda in Iraq. The family also includes Mohammed Deif (Mohammed Diab Ibrahim al-Masri, 1965-2024), the late commander of Hamas's military wing who was killed in an Israeli airstrike. These represent separate family lines that happen to share the Al Masri surname, reflecting their Egyptian heritage or connections.
data.description: Al Masri represents multiple distinct family lineages across the Middle East, including prominent Palestinian business families and various militant figures, united primarily by the surname meaning 'Egyptian' in Arabic.
data.originStory: Multiple origins depending on the branch - Palestinian Al Masris trace to al-Qubeiba near Ramleh in historical Palestine, while other branches originate directly from Egypt
data.modernStatus: The Palestinian branch includes major business leaders like Munib al-Masri (oil fortune, Nablus stock exchange) and Bashar Masri (founder of Rawabi city, Massar International), with connections to Arab Bank and various regional investments
data.nameEtymology: Al-Masri means 'the Egyptian' in Arabic, indicating Egyptian origin or connection

migrationPath.description: Palestinian branch fled during 1948 Palestine war

data.history: The research provided focuses on modern individuals using 'al-Qurashi' as a nom de guerre or religious identifier, claiming descent from the Quraysh tribe of Muhammad. Abu Ibrahim al-Hashimi al-Qurashi (real name: Amir Mohammed Abdul Rahman al-Mawli al-Salbi) was born in 1976 near Mosul, Iraq, and became the second caliph of ISIS from 2019-2022. His predecessor Abu Bakr al-Baghdadi (real name: Ibrahim Awwad Ibrahim Ali al-Badri) also claimed Quraysh lineage and led ISIS from 2010-2019. Both used 'al-Qurashi' to legitimize their caliphate claims, as Islamic tradition requires caliphs to descend from Muhammad's Quraysh tribe. However, experts noted that al-Qurashi's actual family (al-Mawla clan) may have been Turkmen rather than Arab, with genealogy expert Nizar al-Saadoun stating most of the al-Mawla clan was Arab and descended from the Abbasid Burisha clan. The research also mentions Anthony-Rawḥ al-Qurashī, an 8th-century Arab Christian martyr from Damascus who was genuinely descended from the Quraysh tribe.
data.description: Al Qurashi refers to claimed descent from the Quraysh tribe of Muhammad, used as a legitimizing identifier by modern extremist leaders and historically by genuine descendants.
data.originStory: The original Quraysh tribe was the tribe of the Islamic prophet Muhammad, based in Mecca. Modern usage of 'al-Qurashi' often represents claimed rather than verified lineage to this tribe for religious legitimacy.
data.modernStatus: The identifier has been misappropriated by extremist leaders to claim religious legitimacy, while genuine historical descendants include early Islamic figures and Christian martyrs.
data.nameEtymology: Al-Qurashi means 'of the Quraysh,' referring to the tribe of the Islamic prophet Muhammad, used to claim legitimacy for caliphate leadership.

data.history: The Al Maghribi family name (meaning "from the Maghreb" or "from the West") encompasses several distinct historical lineages across the Islamic world, primarily connected by their North African or western origins. The most prominent historical figures include Ibn Sa'id al-Maghribi (1213-1286), an Arab geographer and historian from al-Andalus who was descended from the Companion of the Prophet Ammar ibn Yasir. His family was from Qal'a Benī Sa'īd near Granada and worked for generations on literary projects, particularly the fifteen-volume al-Mughrib fī ḥulā l-Maghrib. Another notable figure was Al-Samawal al-Maghribi (c.1130-1180), a mathematician and physician born to a Jewish family of Moroccan origin whose father was rabbi Yehuda ibn Abūn from Morocco. Al-Husayn ibn Ali al-Maghribi was a Persian-origin statesman whose family acquired the nisbah "al-Maghribi" from heading the "Bureau of the West" (diwan al-maghrib) in the Abbasid administration. The family served various dynasties including the Abbasids, Ikhshidids, and Hamdanids, with Al-Husayn eventually settling in Aleppo as secretary until his death around 965. His descendants continued in government service until a massacre in 1010, with only his grandson Abu'l-Qasim al-Husayn surviving to serve Buyid, Marwanid and Uqaylid courts until 1027.
data.description: Al Maghribi represents multiple historical family lineages distinguished by their North African or western Islamic origins, including prominent scholars, mathematicians, and government administrators who served various Islamic dynasties from al-Andalus to the Abbasid Caliphate.
data.originStory: The various Al Maghribi lineages originated from different parts of the western Islamic world - Ibn Sa'id's family from al-Andalus near Granada with claimed descent from Companion Ammar ibn Yasir, Al-Samawal's family from Morocco, and Al-Husayn's Persian family acquiring the name through administrative service in Baghdad's Bureau of the West.
data.modernStatus: The research does not provide information about any current GCC-based Al Maghribi family's modern business or political activities.
data.nameEtymology: Al-Maghribi derives from Arabic meaning "from the Maghreb" or "from the West," referring to the western regions of the Islamic world including North Africa and al-Andalus.

data.history: The Al Daghistani family encompasses several distinct lineages of Dagestan origin who migrated to different parts of the Middle East during the 19th and 20th centuries. The most documented branches include influential figures in Iraq, Syria, and Cyprus. Murad al-Daghistani (1917-1984) was born in Mosul, Iraq, to Abdul-Hamit Ajamat, a Kumyk engineer who participated in building the Iraqi railroad. This Iraqi branch represents one lineage of the family that settled in Mesopotamia during the early 20th century. A separate and more prominent religious lineage is represented by Abdullah al-Faiz ad-Daghistani (1891-1973), born in the North Caucasian region of Dagestan within the Russian Empire. His family emigrated to the Ottoman Empire in the late 1890s, initially settling in Bursa before moving to the village of Reşadiye in Yalova Province. This village was established on land granted by the sultan and populated by Daghistani refugees affected by the War of '93 and the uprising against the Russian Empire. After various relocations including Egypt and Syria, Abdullah eventually established himself in Damascus, where he founded the first tekke for his branch of the Naqshbandi order and became the 39th Shaykh in the Naqshbandi Haqqani Silsila. His influence extended globally through his student Nazim al-Haqqani, who was born in Cyprus and became a prominent international Sufi leader, demonstrating the family's continued religious and cultural impact across multiple generations and geographical regions.
data.description: Al Daghistani represents multiple family lineages originating from Dagestan in the North Caucasus, with documented branches settling in Iraq, Syria, and Cyprus during the 19th-20th centuries. The family is notable for producing influential religious leaders in the Naqshbandi Sufi tradition and cultural figures such as pioneering photographers.
data.originStory: The Al Daghistani family originates from the North Caucasian region of Dagestan, then part of the Russian Empire. Multiple family branches emigrated during the late 19th and early 20th centuries due to political upheaval, including the War of '93 and uprisings against the Russian Empire, settling in various parts of the Ottoman Empire and later Iraq.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains significant religious influence through the Naqshbandi Haqqani Sufi order, with burial shrines and mosques in Damascus, Syria, and continued international following through spiritual lineages.
data.nameEtymology: Al Daghistani derives from Dagestan (meaning 'land of mountains' in Turkic languages), the North Caucasian region from which the family originated.

data.history: Based on the extensive research provided, there is no direct information about the Al Habashi family (id: al_habashi) as a GCC family lineage. The research contains detailed information about Al-Ahbash, a Lebanese-based Sufi religious movement founded in the mid-1980s that follows Ethiopian scholar Abdullah al-Harari, but this appears to be unrelated to a Gulf family genealogy. The research also includes biographical information about Egyptian pilot Gameel Al-Batouti and extensive historical documentation about Yemeni tribes, but none of this material establishes any connection to an Al Habashi family in the GCC region. Without specific documentation linking these research sources to the Al Habashi family as a Gulf genealogical entity, no verified historical narrative can be constructed.
data.description: No verified information available about Al Habashi as a GCC family lineage based on the provided research sources.

data.history: The Al Bakkal family name derives from the Turkish word 'bakkal,' which refers to traditional small retail shops specializing in non-perishable or semi-fresh food items, canned goods, beverages, and household cleaning products. In Turkey, bakkals have historically served as neighborhood convenience stores, typically family-owned and operated businesses that provide everyday necessities to residents within specific neighborhoods. Unlike supermarkets, bakkals often allowed customers to purchase goods on credit, maintaining informal ledgers known as credit notebooks (veresiye defteri). The bakkal has traditionally held a central place in Turkish neighborhood life, functioning not only as a point of sale but also as a social hub where neighbors interact. In recent decades, the number of bakkals has declined significantly due to the rise of large supermarket chains and discount retailers, though they remain symbolic of Turkey's small-scale entrepreneurship and continue to operate in many neighborhoods, particularly in less urbanized areas.
data.description: Al Bakkal is a family name connected to the Turkish term 'bakkal,' referring to traditional neighborhood convenience store owners and operators in Turkey.
data.originStory: The family name originates from the Turkish occupational term 'bakkal,' indicating ancestors who operated traditional neighborhood convenience stores that served as both retail establishments and social gathering places in Turkish communities.
data.nameEtymology: The name 'Al Bakkal' derives from the Turkish word 'bakkal,' meaning a small traditional retail shop owner or convenience store operator.

data.history: The Al Muhaiiri (also spelled Al Muhairi) family appears in the research primarily through Khalifa bin Butti Al Muhairi, an Emirati billionaire who married Ameera al-Taweel in September 2018 in Paris. The research provides limited information about the family's historical background, origins, or broader genealogy. While the family name suggests Emirati origins given the 'Al' prefix common to Gulf Arab families, no specific tribal affiliations, migration patterns, or historical establishment details are provided in the available research. The family's current prominence appears to be primarily through business success in the UAE, though specific business ventures or sectors are not detailed in the provided materials.

data.history: The spice trade in GCC souks has ancient origins, with historical records documenting bazaars existing in the Persian Gulf region as early as 3000 BCE. In pre-Islamic Arabia, temporary seasonal markets like Suq 'Adan became specifically known for their spices and perfumes, establishing the foundation for specialized spice trading families. During the Islamic period starting in 622 CE, permanent marketplaces developed with specialized sections, including dedicated spice souks that promoted competition among sellers and helped buyers compare prices. Merchants specialized in spice trading were organized into guilds that provided support and represented their interests with authorities. The families operating in spice souks developed expertise passed down through generations, with many having origins in India, Persia, and other regions along ancient caravan trade routes. In Dubai specifically, the spice souk developed alongside the textile and gold souks in the historic Deira neighborhood, benefiting from the city's role as a major Persian Gulf trading hub since at least 1799, with the creek serving as a natural harbor for cargo ships from across the region.
data.description: Traditional merchant families specializing in spice trading within the historic souks of GCC cities, particularly concentrated in areas like Dubai's Spice Souk in Deira, organized into guilds and operating specialized market sections.
data.modernStatus: Continue to operate traditional spice trading businesses in historic souk areas, now primarily serving both local customers and the tourism industry as souks have become major tourist attractions while maintaining their commercial functions.
data.nameEtymology: The term 'souk' derives from Arabic sūq, borrowed from Aramaic šūqā meaning 'street, market', itself a loanword from Akkadian sūqu meaning 'street'

data.history: The Shindagha families are centered around the Al Maktoum ruling family of Dubai, with their historic seat at Sheikh Saeed Al Maktoum House established around 1896 in the Al Shindagha area along Dubai Creek. The area served as the residential quarters and seat of power for the Al Maktoum family, with Sheikh Saeed bin Maktoum Al Maktoum ruling Dubai until his death in 1958. His son Rashid bin Saeed Al Maktoum then acceded as ruler, having effectively managed Dubai's affairs since the late 1930s despite British pressure to force his father's abdication. Under Rashid's leadership from 1958-1990, Dubai transformed from a small creek settlement into a modern commercial hub and port city. The Al Shindagha area was developed through land reclamation using material dredged from Dubai Creek between 1958-1959, with reclaimed land sold to help pay for the dredging operation. Sheikh Rashid married Sheikha Latifa bint Hamdan Al Nahyan, daughter of a former Abu Dhabi ruler who had fled to Dubai after her father's murder, producing nine children who continued the family's political leadership. The area remains significant as the historic birthplace of Rashid's sons and grandsons, including current ruler Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum.
data.description: The Shindagha families are the Al Maktoum ruling family of Dubai, historically based in the Al Shindagha peninsula area along Dubai Creek since 1896. They have ruled Dubai continuously, transforming it from a small trading settlement into a major commercial hub and emirate within the UAE.
data.modernStatus: The Al Maktoum family continues to rule Dubai as one of the seven emirates of the UAE. Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum has been ruler of Dubai since 2006 and serves as UAE Vice President and Prime Minister. The family maintains significant business interests and has developed Dubai into a major international commercial and tourism center.

data.history: The Maraziq (singular Al Marzooqi or Al Marzouqi) is a tribe of the United Arab Emirates traditionally associated with the settlement of Rams, north of Ras Al Khaimah. Together with the Tunaij tribe, they were connected to this historic settlement where the Maraziq population settled the western area known as the Bani Humud neighbourhood (freej) between Rams and Hulaylah. The late Islamic township of Rams consisted primarily of khaimahs - traditional small, stone-walled houses with gabled barasti (palm-frond) roofs. The Maraziq population had left the Rams area by the early 1950s and are today dispersed throughout the UAE. The Bani Humud area of Rams continued to be known as the Marzouqi neighbourhood even after the Maraziq families had departed. The tribe is believed to have originally migrated from Arabia and adopted the name of the district they settled in Rams, which was originally populated by Iranians.
data.description: A UAE tribe traditionally associated with the settlement of Rams in northern Ras Al Khaimah, believed to have migrated from Arabia and dispersed throughout the UAE by the early 1950s.
data.originStory: The Maraziq are believed to have originally migrated from Arabia and took the name of the district they settled in Rams, which was originally populated by Iranians.
data.modernStatus: The family is dispersed throughout the UAE, with notable contemporary figures including engineer Reem Al Marzouqi, the first female UAE citizen to receive a US patent for automotive innovation.
data.nameEtymology: The name is believed to derive from the district of Rams where they settled, which was originally populated by Iranians.

data.history: The Al Haili family is associated with the village of Haili (Al Hailah) in the Al-Buraimi oasis region of northern Oman. According to historical sources, in the pre-Saudi era, the Al-Azaznah tribe held authority over Al Hailah village, which was one of nine villages that comprised the larger Tawam oasis area. The oasis region, historically known as 'Tawam' and 'Al Jaw,' consisted of nine villages including Sa'ara, Hamasah, Haili, Al Jaimi, Al Mawijai, Al Mu'tarid, Al Jahili, Al Ain, and Al Qutayrah. The area came under various tribal and political influences over time, with the Al-Buraimi oasis being abandoned around 700 C.E. before being resettled. The region later came under the influence of Al Saud and experienced various power struggles between different tribal groups and ruling families, including conflicts involving the Al-Azaznah tribe that controlled the Haili area.
data.description: The Al Haili family is connected to the village of Haili (Al Hailah) in the Al-Buraimi oasis region of northern Oman, which was historically controlled by the Al-Azaznah tribe.

data.history: The research reveals Ali Mohamed Mattar as a prominent Salafist politician representing the Asalah party in Bahrain's Chamber of Deputies. He emerged as one of Asalah's most active MPs, carving out a distinctive legislative profile through controversial religious-based proposals. Between 2006-2010, Mattar proposed legislation to ban sorcery, citing the proliferation of fortune tellers and black magic practitioners across the GCC region. He also advocated for the introduction of Sharia Law, including corporal punishment for thieves and blood money systems for accidental killings. His legislative activism positioned him as a significant voice within Bahrain's Sunni Islamist political movement, often clashing with government positions and human rights advocates. The research also mentions other Mattar family connections, including Matar Matar who served as president of the Bahrain Youth Center (Al Wefaq's youth wing) before becoming an MP, and references to the Mattar name in Singapore's MRT system and Palestinian contexts, though these appear to be separate family lines.
data.description: The Mattar family includes prominent Bahraini politicians, notably Ali Mohamed Mattar, a Salafist MP known for proposing religious legislation including bans on sorcery and implementation of Sharia Law.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains political prominence in Bahrain through the Asalah party, with Ali Mohamed Mattar serving as an active MP in the Chamber of Deputies, focusing on religious legislation and Salafist political positions.

data.history: The research presents multiple distinct Al Khalil families across different regions with no established connections. The most documented lineage is from Southern Lebanon, where the al-Khalil family rose from grain merchants to feudal landlords (Zu'ama) in Tyre during the 1858 Ottoman Land reforms. This Lebanese branch was reportedly connected to the Zayn family in Nabatieh and allied with the Sidon-based Osseirans through marriage. They dominated Tyre politics for over a century, engaging in fierce rivalry with the Al-As'ad clan of the Ali al-Saghir dynasty. The family's political prominence peaked under Kazem al-Khalil (1901-1990), who served as a Lebanese parliamentarian and seven-time minister. Another separate Al Khalil family exists as an Arabic tribe in Jammain village, Nablus district, West Bank, belonging to the mother tribe of Al Zeitawi, which reportedly descended from those who came from Mecca and are believed to be direct descendants of Muhammad. A third distinct family line includes Khalil Al-Zayani from Saudi Arabia's Eastern Province, residing in Dammam city. These appear to be entirely separate lineages sharing only the common Arabic surname Al Khalil.
data.description: Multiple distinct Al Khalil families exist across the Arab world, including Lebanese feudal lords from Tyre, Palestinian tribal members in the West Bank, and Saudi Arabian families in the Eastern Province, with no established genealogical connections between the branches.
data.originStory: The Lebanese branch reportedly descended from the Zayn family in Nabatieh, while the Palestinian branch belongs to the Al Zeitawi tribe that came from Mecca and claims descent from Muhammad. The Saudi branch's origins are not specified in the research.
data.modernStatus: The Lebanese branch continues political involvement through Ali Hassan Khalil (Amal Movement parliamentarian) and cultural activities via Maha al-Khalil Chalabi (UNESCO Goodwill Ambassador). The Saudi branch includes former footballer and coach Khalil Al-Zayani.

data.history: The Al Majed family appears to have diverse origins and branches across the Gulf region, with historical presence in Bahrain and other areas. In 18th century Bahrain, Sheikh Mohammed ibn Abdullah Al Majed served as the spiritual leader of Bahrain during a period of Persian rule and political instability. He notably used Huwala forces to combat attacks from the Utub tribes in the early 18th century. The family name appears across multiple contexts in modern times, from football to various professional fields. Contemporary members include notable figures like footballer Majed Abdullah (born 1959 in Jeddah), who became one of Saudi Arabia's greatest strikers, and various other professionals across the Gulf states. The research suggests the Al Majed name has been present in the region for centuries, though specific migration patterns and tribal origins are not clearly documented in the available sources.

data.history: The Al Wadaei family appears to be a Bahraini family with members involved in human rights activism and political dissent. Based on the research, Sayed Ahmed Alwadaei emerged as a prominent human rights activist who was involved in the 2011 Arab Spring protests in Bahrain. Following his participation in the pro-democracy movement, he was jailed and tortured by Bahraini authorities, bearing a prominent scar on his forehead from police brutality. He subsequently fled Bahrain around 2012 and established refugee status in the UK, where he became director of advocacy at the UK-based Bahrain Institute for Rights and Democracy (BIRD). The Bahraini government stripped him of his nationality in 2015, rendering him and his UK-born daughter stateless as retaliation for his activism. His case became emblematic of the broader persecution faced by Bahraini opposition figures, with authorities targeting his family members who remained in Bahrain, including the detention of his wife and infant son in 2016, and the sentencing of three of his relatives to three years in prison on terrorism charges in 2017. After years of legal challenges and threats of legal action, Alwadaei was finally granted UK citizenship in 2024.
data.description: A Bahraini family known primarily through human rights activist Sayed Ahmed Alwadaei, who fled Bahrain following the 2011 Arab Spring and became a prominent voice for Bahraini human rights from exile in the UK.
data.modernStatus: The family is represented by Sayed Ahmed Alwadaei, director of advocacy at the Bahrain Institute for Rights and Democracy, operating as a human rights defender from the UK after being stripped of Bahraini citizenship in 2015.

data.history: The Al Ghali family traces its origins to Egypt, specifically to Boutros Ghali (1846-1910), who was born in Kiman-al-'Arus village in Beni Suef to a Coptic Christian family. His father was Ghali Nayruz, steward of Prince Mustafa Fadil. Boutros Ghali became a prominent Egyptian politician, serving as Prime Minister from 1908 until his assassination in 1910. The family established itself as one of Egypt's most influential Coptic political dynasties through successive generations of high-ranking government officials and diplomats. The most internationally renowned member was Boutros Boutros-Ghali (1922-2016), grandson of the original Boutros Ghali, who served as Egypt's acting foreign minister and later became the sixth Secretary-General of the United Nations from 1992-1996. The family name became synonymous with Egyptian diplomacy and international relations, with multiple members serving in ministerial positions including foreign affairs, finance, and agriculture throughout the 20th and early 21st centuries.
data.description: A prominent Egyptian Coptic Christian political family originating from Beni Suef, known for producing high-ranking government officials including a Prime Minister and UN Secretary-General. The family has maintained significant influence in Egyptian politics and diplomacy for over a century.
data.originStory: The Al Ghali family originated in Egypt with Boutros Ghali (1846-1910), born to Ghali Nayruz in the village of Kiman-al-'Arus in Beni Suef. The family rose to prominence through Boutros Ghali's political career, beginning as a clerk in the Mixed Court in 1875 and culminating in his appointment as Prime Minister in 1908.
data.modernStatus: The family continues to hold influence in Egyptian politics and international affairs. Recent notable members include Youssef Boutros Ghali who served as Minister of Finance from 2004-2011, and Teymour Boutros-Ghali who became an internet entrepreneur.
